films.title: The House of Mirth
films.synopsis: Terence Davies reaches his mature style in The House of Mirth through Edith Wharton’s novelistic New York, where power and money circulate in guarded social environments.

films.title: The Razor’s Edge
films.synopsis: A footprint in ink and a handprint in blood—the poles of art and war tug at the fabric of life in Lebanese filmmaker Jocelyne Saab’s fiction debut The Razor’s Edge, set amid a crumbling Beirut ravaged by years of civil combat.

films.title: Marnie
films.synopsis: Alfred Hitchcock’s final study of obsession, Marnie, is an unsettling film about what it takes for two class-divided people to agree on common terms: theft, marriage, crime, sex, and even the colour red.

films.title: Marie Antoinette
films.synopsis: A dreamy portrait of any-era girlhood alienation, Coppola’s forte—Marie Antoinette, the writer-director’s follow-up to Lost in Translation, has amassed a considerable cult of appreciation in the 20 years since its divisive Cannes debut.

films.title: Sunset Song
films.synopsis: Davies’s Sunset Song is seemingly placid, evoking the paintings of Vilhelm Hammershøi, but the plot relentlessly closes in on Chris; before she has the chance to understand her own desires, she is forced into impossible, traditional roles.

films.title: The Green Ray
films.synopsis: Forty years on and as radiant as ever, The Green Ray, Eric Rohmer’s enchanting ode to summertime sadness, remains one of his most cherished (and relatable) masterpieces.

films.title: A Quiet Passion
films.synopsis: Terence Davies’s A Quiet Passion reconstructs the personhood and artistry of the poet Emily Dickinson (Cynthia Nixon) to observe how the writer might have sharpened her piercing voice on the page in the absence of recognition or assurance.

films.title: Take Care of My Cat
films.synopsis: An unusually subtle and lived-in portrait of young women and their aspirations, Jeong Jaeeun’s Take Care of My Cat was ahead of its time—a cult classic that is now finally receiving its due.

films.title: With Hasan in Gaza
films.synopsis: Constructed from three MiniDV tapes shot in Gaza in 2001 and rediscovered years later, Palestinian filmmaker and visual artist Kamal Aljafari’s With Hasan in Gaza transforms recovered footage into a profound cinematic meditation on memory and loss.

films.title: The Currents
films.synopsis: In The Currents, director Milagros Mumenthaler fuses the prosaic experience of reaching the apex of a career with a thrillingly modernist mystery of identity and knowability.

films.title: Anne of the Indies
films.synopsis: Jacques Tourneur’s Anne of the Indies features a rare leading action role for a woman in the 1950s as part of a story of betrayal, Blackbeard, bear wrestling, and big, blazingly beautiful battle sequences!

films.title: Notes in Origin: The Films of Ellie Epp
films.synopsis: The Iris Film Collective is thrilled to present this retrospective featuring Ellie Epp’s four 16mm films (1975–1996) in new digital restorations and five more recent digital video works never before shown in Vancouver.

films.title: There Are No Words
films.synopsis: Despite its title, Min Sook Lee’s There Are No Words affirms that though there may be no words to describe the depth of one’s grief, the uneasy journey to preserve a loved one’s memory is a worthy, even empowering, cause.

films.title: Two Seasons, Two Strangers
films.synopsis: Already a critics’ favourite in his native Japan, Miyake Sho should see his international following surge thanks to Two Seasons, Two Strangers, a Locarno-crowned adaptation of two manga by Tsuge Yoshiharu.

films.title: All the Long Nights
films.synopsis: Two coworkers with socially isolating conditions discover a sense of belonging in Miyake Sho’s All the Long Nights, a quiet drama that gathers its emotional resonance in small, calculated strides.

films.title: Benediction
films.synopsis: Terence Davies’s Benediction is an artist’s biography, a bitterly tinged chronicle of romance, and an account of anti-war refusal; it is also a tantalizing mirror that at times resembles a self-portrait.

films.title: The Forgotten Reels of Nunavut’s Animation Workshop
films.synopsis: The newly uncovered reels in this program mark a pivotal moment in Nunavut’s film history, revealing the first wave of local filmmakers to move beyond conventional storytelling toward experimental, abstract, and satirical forms.

films.title: Atanarjuat: The Fast Runner
films.synopsis: Zacharias Kunuk’s legendary work Atanarjuat: The Fast Runner is the first feature made entirely in the Inuktitut language, and is written, produced, directed, and acted by an almost all-Inuit ensemble.

films.title: Small, Slow But Steady
films.synopsis: Drawn from the memoir of boxer Ogasawara Keiko, Miyake Sho’s understated, COVID-set sports drama Small, Slow But Steady was a festival standout and a critical darling domestically, earning Kishii Yukino a Japanese Academy Award for her performance.

films.title: All About Lily Chou-Chou
films.synopsis: Iwai Shunji said of his digital epic that the film is partly based on the unforgettable experience of witnessing bullying: “The worse your experience has been, the bluer the sky looks. There’s a lot of that in All About Lily Chou-Chou.”

films.title: The Taste of Tea
films.synopsis: Imagination is the modus operandi guiding Ishii Katsuhito’s The Taste of Tea, a whimsical chronicle of a quirky, millennial Japanese family.

films.title: Love Letter
films.synopsis: Iwai Shunji's feature debut Love Letter is still, to many, his most perfect film, one infused with both classical and ’90s-specific ideas of melodrama, and a tonal range that capably switches between haunted seriousness and lively comedy.

films.title: April Story
films.synopsis: Between his wildly ambitious Swallowtail Butterfly and All About Lily Chou-Chou, Iwai Shunji made April Story, a wondrous film—just barely over an hour—that builds an entire world without any genre revision or tragic violence.

films.title: Hana & Alice
films.synopsis: In Hana & Alice, as in a silent film comedy, an imagined story is implanted, takes root, and grows into a domino effect of additional lies, card tricks, comical confusions, and, in Iwai’s unique touch, tangential plotlines of beauty and heartbreak.

films.title: The Case of Hana & Alice
films.synopsis: The Case of Hana & Alice, a work of rotoscope animation, is set as a prequel, in which Iwai Shunji introduces a strong, almost paranoid tone of small-town superstition, which sets the two leads off on a (relatively harmless) detective hunt.

films.title: An Evening with Tilby/Forbis
films.synopsis: The Cinematheque and Emily Carr University of Art + Design are pleased to present an evening with award-winning animators Wendy Tilby and Amanda Forbis, who met as classmates studying film, video, and animation at ECU in the mid-1980s.

films.title: L’enfance nue
films.synopsis: L’enfance nue, a debut of staggering intention and execution, was made when Maurice Pialat was at the relatively mature age of 43. Screens with Pialat’s short film L’amour existe.

films.title: Graduate First
films.synopsis: Utterly avoiding the tired clichés of the typical teen film, Maurice Pialat’s Graduate First is a minutely observed, uncommonly honest profile of disaffected French youth in a northern provincial city.

films.title: La maison des bois
films.synopsis: La maison des bois stands as one of the greatest achievements in the history of television as well as legendary director Maurice Pialat’s magnum opus.

films.title: We Will Not Grow Old Together
films.synopsis: We Will Not Grow Old Together, Maurice Pialat’s remarkable second feature, is an uncompromising study of the break-up of a relationship. Preceded by Pialat’s short film Janine.

films.title: Smoke Signals
films.synopsis: Iconic cannot begin to describe Smoke Signals, which is often recognized as the first fiction feature written, directed, and co-produced by Indigenous Peoples to be distributed nationally and internationally.

films.title: Mysterious Skin
films.synopsis: No cinematic treatment of trauma has eclipsed the horrific and sensitive Mysterious Skin, a narrative unlike any other in Gregg Araki’s filmography.

films.title: Three Bewildered People in the Night
films.synopsis: A transmission from the “totally hostile and irrational universe” of Los Angeles, Gregg Araki’s rarely screened no-budget debut Three Bewildered People in the Night is an emotionally transparent work of inspired self-definition.

films.title: The Mouth Agape
films.synopsis: The Mouth Agape, Maurice Pialat’s third feature, is an unflinching account of a middle-aged woman’s discovery that she has cancer and the effect this has on her family.

films.title: Macho Dancer
films.synopsis: Incendiary Filipino auteur Lino Brocka took the softcore “bomba” genre to queerer and more socially trenchant frontiers with Macho Dancer, part gay erotica, part Manila-underbelly exposé, all provocative enough to trigger ample censorship edits.

films.title: Alice in Wonderland
films.director: Clyde Geronimi, Wilfred Jackson, Hamilton Luske
films.synopsis: Walt Disney read Lewis Carroll’s Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland in school, and the idea to adapt it followed him through decades of his career. After he saw Mary Blair’s bold, modern drawings, he saw a way forward to Alice in Wonderland.

films.title: Kiarostami at Work
films.synopsis: To mark the tenth anniversary of his death, Pelan is honoured to celebrate the filmmaker’s life and work by presenting the Canadian premiere of Kiarostami at Work, directed by celebrated photographer Seifollah Samadian, his longtime friend.

films.title: Police
films.synopsis: Maurice Pialat’s big-budget Police is a violent, metaphysical, unconventional policier featuring a viscous turn from Gérard Depardieu, named Best Actor at Venice for his performance.

films.title: À nos amours
films.synopsis: Maurice Pialat’s searing domestic drama À nos amours features a remarkably intense performance by a 16-year-old Sandrine Bonnaire (in her screen debut) as Suzanne, an adolescent girl searching for love.

films.title: Loulou
films.synopsis: In Maurice Pialat’s unsettling tale of erotic obsession Loulou, Isabelle Huppert plays Nelly, a refined bourgeois woman who dumps her husband for unbridled sex and utter irresponsibility with Loulou, a layabout played by Gérard Depardieu.

films.title: Just a bit of action: Kerr Holden and Minki Hong
films.synopsis: From Granville Street to Seoul’s “B-rate” cinemas and the depths of an athlete’s physical injury, these documentaries by Kerr Holden and Minki Hong are threaded by the desire for just a bit of action.

films.title: Under the Sun of Satan
films.synopsis: Winner of the Palme d’Or at Cannes in 1987, Maurice Pialat’s Under the Sun of Satan is a dark and demanding study of faith and evil that recalls the work of Carl Theodor Dreyer and Robert Bresson while anticipating Bruno Dumont.

films.title: Niagara
films.synopsis: Marilyn Monroe and Joseph Cotten are a maniacally mismatched couple vacationing on the Ontario side of Niagara Falls in Henry Hathaway’s delectably tawdry thriller Niagara.

films.title: The Killers
films.synopsis: Directed by Hollywood luminary Robert Siodmak (Criss Cross, Phantom Lady), The Killers is one of film noir’s defining works—a master class in the criminality, corruption, and cynicism of the American-bred genre.

films.title: Kiss Me Deadly
films.synopsis: Kiss Me Deadly, masterfully directed by Hollywood subversive Robert Aldrich, is a nihilistic, nuclear-age noir of legendary repute.

films.title: Murder by Contract
films.synopsis: A noir of the most austere and existential variety, Murder by Contract follows a suave, remorseless hitman (Vince Edwards) who dispatches strangers for the competitive salary and the principled pleasure of a job done right.

films.title: Pitfall
films.synopsis: In Pitfall, André de Toth’s svelte noir, Dick Powell is John Forbes, a family and insurance man dreaming of shaking off the doldrums of middle-class mundanity. He gets his wish...

films.title: The Sniper
films.synopsis: The misogyny of film noir is distilled and driven to its darkest conclusion in The Sniper, Edward Dmytryk’s serial-killer thriller and an evolutionary step between Lang’s M and Hitchcock’s Psycho.

films.title: Van Gogh
films.synopsis: Maurice Pialat’s follow up to the Palme d’Or-winning Under the Sun of Satan was this nuanced, naturalistic account of the last days of Vincent Van Gogh.

films.title: The Most Terrible Time in My Life
films.synopsis: Gleefully cinephilic and self-aware, The Most Terrible Time in My Life, the inaugural entry in Hayashi Kaizo’s Maiku Hama trilogy, sets down the rules of play (emphasis on play) for the entire pastiche-laden neo-noir saga.

films.title: Bamako
films.synopsis: Bamako, named for the capital of Mali where it takes place, literally puts the global financial system on trial.

films.title: The Beirut Trilogy
films.synopsis: Jocelyne Saab’s “Beirut Trilogy” is an extraordinary portrait of life during wartime, set in the ruins of Saab’s no-longer-recognizable birthplace.

films.title: Singing Wings
films.synopsis: Singing Wings is a poetic and deeply human documentary set in a picturesque Kurdish village in Iran, where migrating storks arrive each winter.

films.title: Of Time and the City
films.synopsis: Terence Davies’s sole nonfiction film, Of Time and the City, is a scathing and completely unbridled memoir of affection and loss for his hometown of Liverpool.

films.title: Living the Land
films.synopsis: Huo Meng was the well-merited winner of Best Director at Berlin for his engrossing third feature Living the Land, a vividly rendered portrait of rural China in the midst of existential change.

films.title: Once Were Warriors
films.synopsis: The acclaimed feature debut of Māori filmmaker Lee Tamahori, Once Were Warriors follows an urban Māori whānau (or family) as they grapple with intergenerational trauma.

films.title: The Neon Bible
films.synopsis: Having pushed his highly controlled yet free-associative style to its zenith in The Long Day Closes, Terence Davies turned for the first time to a continuous narrative in his adaptation of John Kennedy Toole’s The Neon Bible.

films.title: Early Cranes
films.synopsis: Masterfully weaving strands of memories and bleak reality together, Bolotbek Shamshiyev brings Early Cranes to life in collaboration with Chingiz Aitmatov, the author of the source novel and arguably the greatest figure in Kyrgyz literature.

films.title: The Touch
films.synopsis: In The Touch, director Amanzhol Aituarov and his Kyrgyz writing partner Bayan Sarygulov turn to a parable of two drifters wandering the bygone Kazakh steppe to reflect on loss in late Soviet Kazakhstan.

films.title: The Terence Davies Trilogy
films.synopsis: The three films that open Terence Davies’s career—Children, Madonna and Child, and Death and Transfiguration—comprise an education, covering his time at the National Film School.

films.title: Distant Voices, Still Lives
films.synopsis: A masterpiece of formal and personal invention, Terence Davies’s Distant Voices, Still Lives takes as its starting point the impossibility of separating events from one another, no matter how extreme.

films.title: The Long Day Closes
films.synopsis: Terence Davies’s most beloved film The Long Day Closes is autobiographical, covering the years of Davies’s adolescence after his abusive father’s death.

films.title: The Deep Blue Sea
films.synopsis: The frame-perfect vision of Terence Davies’s early personal films can overshadow the inspired work in adaptation and biography that followed, of which the standout might be The Deep Blue Sea.

films.title: Shadowbox
films.synopsis: Rooted in the desire to break cycles of domestic crisis, Shadowbox is a meditation on the complexities of gendered labour, mental health, and family systems.

films.title: The Ladykillers
films.synopsis: A heist thriller where the pinnacle scene is an extended, courteous invitation to afternoon tea, The Ladykillers is full of danger, yet it’s a danger constantly undercut by an impish delight in contrasts.

films.title: Without Fear
films.synopsis: In Without Fear, Ali Khamraev approaches the especially delicate subject of women caught in an ideological crossfire.

films.title: A New Love in Tokyo
films.synopsis: Takahashi Banmei’s A New Love in Tokyo is a film about the continuities between nightlife and working hours, sex work and relationships, theatre and job performance for two young women and the friends, johns, and subs they know.

films.title: 100 Sunset
films.synopsis: Kunsang Kyirong’s first feature 100 Sunset is a rich and intricate mystery that deals in crime, secrecy, and masked desire, whether between two women or among an entire community.

films.title: Ombres de soie
films.synopsis: Inspired by Maya Deren and Marguerite Duras, Mary Stephen made Shades of Silk, a singular film of tactile, erotic suggestion and bold, asynchronous craft that has been impossible to see until its recent unearthing and restoration.

films.title: The Headless Woman
films.synopsis: The Headless Woman is the mesmerizing third feature by Lucrecia Martel, and more than likely her chef d’oeuvre.

films.title: Our Land
films.synopsis: Our Land, the first feature documentary from Lucrecia Martel, again proves the Argentine auteur’s slow-bake methods yield unparalleled results.

films.title: Keys to Kingdoms: The Legacy of Bud Osborn
films.synopsis: Nathaniel Geary and Ryan Sudds have made many friends and a few films in Vancouver’s Downtown East Side (DTES). These films emphasize the continuum of artistic and cultural acts that have been articulated across decades of struggle and solidarity.

films.title: No Home Movie
films.synopsis: No Home Movie, the farewell film of Chantal Akerman, is a portrait of the director’s mother in the final months of her life.

films.title: The Damned
films.synopsis: Luchino Visconti’s The Damned operates as a cinematic archaeology of decadence, excavating a civilization already functionally dead—its institutions moving with the eerie persistence of a corpse animated by habit, violence, and self-preserving rot.

films.title: Perforations: The Films of Gordon Matta-Clark
films.synopsis: This program collects six of Gordon Matta-Clark’s films shot between 1972 and 1976, documenting four building cuts, one formal experiment, and one car crash.

films.title: Kontinental ’25
films.synopsis: In a quickly assembled, iPhone-shot film like Kontinental ’25, Radu Jude never neglects the strong and complex central performance by Eszter Tompa as bailiff Orsolya, but he mainly works from a collage of online detritus and historical references.

films.title: A Couch in New York
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s work in comic traditions reached its most distilled and popular expression in the perfectly symmetrical A Couch in New York.

films.title: Paul
films.synopsis: Denis Côté’s portrait is never sensational or salacious; with a tender observational style we meet Paul as he really is.

films.title: Everybody in the Place: An Incomplete History of Britain 1984–1992
films.synopsis: Everybody in the Place is a documentary by the artist Jeremy Deller. It summarizes the explosion of acid house and rave in the UK as a reaction to wider and deeper fault lines in British culture, cutting across class, identity, and geography.

films.title: Watership Down
films.synopsis: Watership Down is a powerful, complicated classic, an odyssey—in a pastoral lineage that includes Bambi, Pom Poko, and Babe—that charts the mixture of safety, peril, and discovery that accompanies leaving one home to find another.

films.title: Yi Yi
films.synopsis: Intimate yet epic, tender but honest, rooted in realism while unafraid to summon magic, Yi Yi is Edward Yang’s beloved final feature, a virtual summation of the director’s signature themes and tonal range.

films.title: Punishment Park
films.synopsis: Punishment Park is the only project British filmmaker Peter Watkins made in the US. It is a pseudo-documentary that imagines President Richard Nixon employing an actual state of emergency bill to extrajudicially interrogate and detain dissidents.

films.title: Portrait of a Young Girl at the End of the 1960s in Brussels + I’m Hungry, I’m Cold
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s Portrait of a Young Girl at the End of the 1960s in Brussels is, like its title’s specificity suggests, as intensely observant and personally detailed as her masterpiece Jeanne Dielman. Screening with  I’m Hungry, I’m Cold.

films.title: Almayer’s Folly
films.synopsis: Akerman’s final work of fiction Almayer’s Folly, based on Joseph Conrad’s debut novel, is something of a sister film to La captive, her entrancing Proust adaptation from a decade earlier.

films.title: La région centrale
films.synopsis: For a 21-year-old Chantal Akerman entering the avant-garde cradle of New York in the early ’70s, watching Michael Snow’s La région centrale was a formative encounter.

films.title: Histoires d’Amérique: Food, Family and Philosophy
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman made several monologue films; Histoires d’Amérique: Food, Family and Philosophy is by far her most varied and curious-minded one.

films.title: La captive
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman entered the new century with a project she’d been circling since the 1970s: a film adaptation of Marcel Proust’s monumental À la recherche du temps perdu.

films.title: War Pony
films.synopsis: War Pony is grit and humour, and it thrives with radical hope. Everything is kind of rundown, worn in a bit, as the characters endure life on the Pine Ridge Rez.

films.title: The Eighties
films.synopsis: The Eighties, a “test run” for Chantal Akerman’s musical Golden Eighties, is in two parts: powerfully edited rehearsal footage recorded on video, and musical sequences shot on film, using a cast and sets that were not yet finalized.

films.title: Dry Leaf
films.synopsis: Dry Leaf, Georgian filmmaker Alexandre Koberidze’s much-anticipated follow-up to his enchanting festival charmer What Do We See When We Look at the Sky? (2021), is among the best films of 2025.

films.title: I Am the Art NOBUO KUBOTA
films.synopsis: I Am the Art NOBUO KUBOTA is a bold, intimate portrait of a Japanese Canadian trailblazer whose creativity has never stopped evolving.

films.title: Sweet Summer Pow Wow
films.synopsis: Set against the vibrancy and community of powwow culture in British Columbia, Sweet Summer Pow Wow is a coming-of-age story about love, responsibility, and choosing one’s own path.

films.title: Golden Eighties
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s Golden Eighties, a deeply personal, totally extroverted musical, is permanently poised to break out—in exquisitely choreographed song-and-dance numbers, nervous breakdowns, or maelstroms of capitalism-enthused monologues.

films.title: D’Est
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s now-entrenched position at the intersection of cinema and art installation first emerged out of the fecundity of D’est, perhaps her most formally significant post-Jeanne Dielman work.

films.title: Les rendez-vous d’Anna
films.synopsis: A forlorn portrait of an Akerman-like artist’s estrangement from the world and others, Les rendez-vous d’Anna is the director’s extraordinary fiction follow-up to Jeanne Dielman and one of great works of the 1970s.

films.title: Toute une nuit
films.synopsis: The stillness of an Edward Hopper painting and the mounting progression of a structural film are married in Chantal Akerman’s Toute une nuit, a secret masterwork in which people meet, part, and reunite over a single hushed evening.

films.title: What Does That Nature Say to You
films.synopsis: Hong Sangsoo’s only picture of 2025, What Does That Nature Say to You, offers a typically unshowy but painfully observant portrait of human folly, here assuming the outward appearance of a meet-the-parents comedy.

films.title: Greek Women Directors × 3
films.synopsis: This program of three short and mid-length works emphasizes women’s subjectivity across decades of historical change. Includes Frieda Liappa’s newly restored I Remember You Leaving All the Time.

films.title: Je tu il elle
films.synopsis: The first narrative feature by Chantal Akerman is a provocative, acutely personal meditation on the need for human contact, and a formative aesthetic precursor to her triumph Jeanne Dielman, released the following year.

films.title: News from Home
films.synopsis: After the incredible achievement of Jeanne Dielman, Chantal Akerman returned to what might be understood as her cinematic birthplace in New York, where she made News from Home.

films.title: Hotel Monterey and Two Early Shorts
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s formative stint in New York (1971–73) culminated in the structuralist masterpiece Hotel Monterey, her first long-form experiment in duration and visual strategy.

films.title: Soldier of Orange
films.synopsis: From the capitulation of Rotterdam in 1940 to the war’s end, Soldier of Orange follows the aristocratic Erik Lanshof (Rutger Hauer) and the way his actions get summed up as heroism.

films.title: Jeanne Dielman, 23 quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles
films.synopsis: Chantal Akerman’s astonishing chef d’oeuvre Jeanne Dielman, made with an all-female crew in 1975, was immediately recognized as a milestone of feminist cinema and is now regularly cited as one of the greatest films ever made.

films.title: Shared Resources
films.synopsis: Foregrounding accessibility as a participatory strategy, Shared Resources is a documentary that is as critical as it is sincere, as formally inventive as its themes are familiar.

films.title: Harvest
films.synopsis: Harvest is the English-language debut of the formidably talented Greek New Wave director Athina Rachel Tsangari, who pivots impressively from the satire of Chevalier and absurdism of Attenberg to this unique period picture.

films.title: BLKNWS: Terms & Conditions
films.synopsis: A dazzling embroidery of ideas on Black history, identity, and aesthetics gives shape to artist Kahlil Joseph’s eagerly awaited debut feature, an expansion of his acclaimed 2019 Venice Biennale installation BLKNWS.

films.title: Head On
films.synopsis: Head On works with the tropes of Greeks in diaspora, such as intragenerational conflict, wise aunts, and even Greek dancing. Yet Ana Kokkinos directs with freshness and originality, and grounds the film in a strong sense of realism.

films.title: In My Parents’ House
films.synopsis: Featuring an evocative lead performance from Schily, In My Parents’ House is a meditation on familial obligation that ponders the question: who bears the responsibility when a loved one resists treatment?

films.title: Panic in the Streets
films.synopsis: Panic in the Streets is a tense, realistic exemplar of director Elia Kazan, praised for its on-location filming in New Orleans, sharp dialogue, strong performances (especially from Jack Palance and Richard Widmark), and unique blend of genres.

films.title: Origin
films.synopsis: Origin is a sprawling, ambitious exploration of racial inequality in contemporary America and its interconnection with global systems of oppression throughout history.

films.title: Tales of Beatrix Potter
films.synopsis: Many of Beatrix Potter’s tales are adapted and mixed into this dance film, including Squirrel Nutkin, the Two Bad Mice, and Jemima Puddle-Duck.

films.title: Kyuka: Before Summer’s End
films.synopsis: An astonishing, stylish, and fresh feature film debut made with cinematic flourishes and editing freedom, Kyuka: Before Summer’s End is an exquisite, immersive experience that has drawn comparisons to splashy debuts like Murina and Aftersun.

films.title: Happy Day
films.synopsis: Happy End, a controversial political allegory, depicts the experience of political prisoners exiled to a small Aegean island during the military junta—and in particular, one prisoner’s refusal to submit.

films.title: Days and Nights in the Forest
films.synopsis: Satyajit Ray’s Days and Nights in the Forest is one of his most wise and invigorating contemporary fables—a film about the roundelay of desire and disillusion, and the great gulf of experience gained from either side of the class divide.

films.title: Electra
films.synopsis: Electra, an exquisite screen adaptation of Euripides’s classic tragedy, is distinguished by its powerful performances including a riveting, magnetic tour de force turn from star Irene Papas (Z).

films.title: Aki
films.synopsis: Aki is an experimental love letter to the land, to Darlene Naponse’s community, and to the cycles of life.

films.title: A History of Violence
films.synopsis: David Cronenberg tackles human nature and the American myth of self-reinvention in A History of Violence, a masterful psychological thriller and one of the best films of the 2000s.

films.title: Safe
films.synopsis: Todd Haynes’s against-the-grain second feature Safe—quite possibly his masterpiece—is one of the great movies of the 1990s.

films.title: Magellan
films.synopsis: Magellan is a major work, one destined to draw greater awareness to its maker Laz Diaz, Philippine cinema’s reigning auteur.

films.title: While the Green Grass Grows: A Diary in Seven Parts
films.synopsis: Filmed over three years, Peter Mettler’s magnum opus While the Green Grass Grows is a sprawling, yet intimate, intercontinental epic that embraces coincidence and elevates the film diary to new heights of innovation.

films.title: Alma’s Rainbow
films.synopsis: In Alma’s Rainbow, Bronx teenager Rainbow (Victoria Gabrielle Platt) defines herself through dance and music, while her mother Alma (Kim Weston-Moran) worries about her daughter’s changing place in the world.

films.title: The Spook Who Sat by the Door
films.synopsis: Embodying and subverting the tenets of Blaxploitation, The Spook Who Sat by the Door is an open-hearted piece of political cinema.

films.title: Northlore + Delta Dawn
films.synopsis: Rescheduled from our “Stories to Share: Respecting the Spirit” program in October, Northlore, an animated and live-action hybrid, screens with the short documentary Delta Dawn.

films.title: collect, release, fall, rise: The Films of Erica Sheu
films.synopsis: In this program, Iris Film Collective presents eight short films and expanded-cinema works by LA-based, Taiwanese artist Erica Sheu (徐璐).

films.title: Gambling, Gods and LSD
films.synopsis: Mettler’s most emblematic work Gambling, Gods and LSD is rigorously associative, traversing diverse continents and landscapes, spiritual convictions and psychological states.

films.title: Fire of Wind
films.synopsis: The grape harvest in Portugal’s Alentejo region provides a rhythmic process, a landscape under piercing, glowing light, and a historical reference point for collective action in Marta Mateus’s debut feature Fire of Wind.

films.title: A Wanderer’s Notebook
films.synopsis: A Wanderer’s Notebook, Naruse Mikio’s sixth and final film based on a Hayashi Fumiko work, serves as a loving paean to the literary icon whose writing underpins the director’s most beloved films.

films.title: Picture of Light
films.synopsis: A journey to civilization in extremis, Picture of Light follows Mettler and a small crew to Churchill, Manitoba, on a pre-digital expedition to photograph the aurora borealis.

films.title: They Are Sacred
films.synopsis: They Are Sacred profiles Dr. Grant Bruno, whose passion for fostering culturally responsive healthcare is not just part of his professional life, it is also his lived reality—his son Anders is autistic.

films.title: Wabbit Twouble and More Looney Tunes
films.synopsis: Here are ten more Looney Tunes cartoons including the first-ever official appearance of Bugs Bunny, and the early, slightly different-looking versions of Sylvester and Tweety Bird!

films.title: Scattered Clouds
films.synopsis: Scattered Clouds, the exquisite last testament of Naruse Mikio, dying of cancer during its production, is a sombre portrait of love’s futility in the inescapable shadow of tragedy.

films.title: Rosa la rose, fille publique
films.synopsis: Rosa la rose, fille publique is one of Paul Vecchiali’s most approachable films, a near-musical display of style that aims to stun.

films.title: Robert Beavers: Program Three
films.synopsis: These final works in Robert Beavers’s defining film cycle My Hand Outstretched to the Winged Distance and Sightless Measure (The Hedge Theater, The Stoas, and The Ground) present something both tactile and elusive.

films.title: Robert Beavers: Program Four
films.synopsis: Robert Beavers’s most recent work, including Pitcher of Colored Light and The Sparrow Dream, shows further transformations in his practice. Each shot refracts an immense scale of consistent attention.

films.title: Robert Beavers: Program One
films.synopsis: In Early Monthly Segments, Robert Beavers’s editing captures the bold, playful energy of someone essaying an entire catalogue of available colours, camera movements, and poses. This pursuit of beauty also appears in Still Light and Wingseed.

films.title: Robert Beavers: Program Two
films.synopsis: Robert Beavers’s From the Notebook of... is an exactly measured, separated, and classified system. In AMOR, Beavers stitches fabric and forking garden paths together via a forceful, enigmatic figuration.

films.title: Yearning
films.synopsis: A tender, touching, and trenchant late work by Naruse, Yearning stars Takamine Hideko in a Locarno-winning performance as hard-working Reiko, who was made a war widow just six months into marriage.

films.title: Wild Reeds
films.synopsis: André Téchiné’s greatest film Wild Reeds is a particularly agile version of adolescence, one where political awareness, sexual awakening, friendly alliances, and music cues—all in flux—test the boundaries of a small, southwestern French town.

films.title: When a Woman Ascends the Stairs
films.synopsis: Described as “the last classic of Japanese cinema’s pre-New Wave golden age,” Naruse Mikio’s When a Woman Ascends the Stairs returns to the melancholic working-class milieu of his earlier Flowing.

films.title: The Lovers on the Bridge
films.synopsis: Leos Carax’s spectacular third feature The Lovers on the Bridge, made in the midst of France’s bicentennial celebrations, is a vivid, visionary valentine to Paris.

films.title: Bye Bye Love
films.synopsis: Considered lost until a recent archival rediscovery, Bye Bye Love is a singular work of independent queer cinema.

films.title: My Stolen Planet
films.synopsis: Through an intimate collage of home videos and found footage, in My Stolen Planet Farahnaz Sharifi contrasts the private spaces where women dance and live freely and the public realm where the hijab is enforced and women’s rights are ignored.

films.title: Boy Meets Girl
films.synopsis: Leos Carax’s first feature Boy Meets Girl was rapturously received: it was widely hailed as one of the most impressive and original debuts in French cinema since Breathless, and raised hopes that a new Godard had emerged!

films.title: Mauvais sang
films.synopsis: Leos Carax described Mauvais sang, his eagerly anticipated follow-up to Boy Meets Girl, as “a film which loves cinema but which does not love the cinema of today.”

films.title: Flowing
films.synopsis: Featuring a trio of superb female actors in the leads, Naruse Mikio’s Flowing was much praised by Japanese critics for its evocation of the geisha world in decline.

films.title: Siksikakowan: The Blackfoot Man
films.synopsis: In Siksikakowan: The Blackfoot Man, filmmaker Sinakson Trevor Solway returns home to his Siksika Nation to document the lives of Blackfoot men as they navigate identity, kinship systems, and the complex expectations of manhood.

films.title: Aggregate Squish: Kasper Feyrer & Kara Ditte Hansen
films.synopsis: “Aggregate Squish” brings together the complementary practices of Kasper Feyrer and Kara Ditte Hansen on 16mm as a playful corpus of cinema.

films.title: Late Chrysanthemums
films.synopsis: One of Naruse’s pantheon films, Late Chrysanthemums, a character-driven tale of four retired geishas, is based on three stories by Hayashi Fumiko.

films.title: Sound of the Mountain
films.synopsis: Held in the highest regard by Naruse admirers, Sound of the Mountain, an exquisitely realized and profoundly moving drama of a failed marriage, is based on one of the major novels of Kawabata Yasunari, winner of the 1968 Nobel Prize in Literature.

films.title: Lightning
films.synopsis: Lightning, Naruse Mikio’s second adaptation of a Hayashi Fumiko novel, is a lively portrait of family dysfunction that takes domestic squabbling to new heights (or perhaps new lows) in the director’s work.

films.title: Viridiana
films.synopsis: After an exile that lasted over two decades, Luis Buñuel was convinced by actor Silvia Pinal and her husband, producer Gustavo Alatriste, to return to Spain to direct a film. The result was the scandalous Viridiana.

films.title: Life After
films.synopsis: In Life After, award-winning director Reid Davenport sets out on a personal investigation from Texas to Ontario, where he meets disabled people who contemplate the thin line between empowered decision and coerced resolve.

films.title: Wife
films.synopsis: Wife, an adaptation of a Hayashi Fumiko novel, must surely count among Naruse’s most unsparing portraits of wedlock come asunder.

films.title: Castle in the Sky
films.synopsis: We’re starting a new year of Film Club by marking the 40th anniversary of the first-ever Studio Ghibli production, Castle in the Sky!

films.title: Repast
films.synopsis: A poignant marital drama, Repast casts Ozu regular Hara Setsuko (who also appears in Naruse’s Sound of the Mountain) as Osaka housewife Michiyo.

films.title: Mother
films.synopsis: Tanaka Kinuyo, the preeminent actor and pioneering female director, made several important films with Naruse Mikio, including the much-loved Mother, one of the director’s most celebrated achievements.

films.title: Floating Clouds
films.synopsis: A tale of amour fou set in the ruins of postwar Tokyo, Floating Clouds is ranked by many as master Naruse Mikio’s pinnacle achievement, and was his greatest popular success in Japan.

films.title: Dance for Camera: Early Works by Charles Atlas
films.synopsis: This program features three early works produced by Charles Atlas in collaboration with dancers, each reflecting the exuberant spirit, canny camerawork, and anti-documentary approach that defines this period in his practice.

films.title: The Battle of Algiers
films.synopsis: One of the great epics of revolutionary political cinema, Gillo Pontecorvo’s legendary The Battle of Algiers grippingly recreates the armed struggle for Algerian independence against France in the 1950s.

films.title: Wrong Husband
films.synopsis: Winner of Best Canadian Feature at TIFF 2025, Zacharias Kunuk’s Wrong Husband is a mythical account of fated love and supernatural intervention—and something of a departure from the director’s customary verité approach.

films.title: Kaili Blues + A Short Story
films.synopsis: After completing his feature-length graduation film Tiger, Bi Gan took four years before he returned with his acclaimed international debut Kaili Blues. The film screens with Bi’s short film A Short Story.

films.title: Long Day’s Journey into Night
films.synopsis: Bi Gan’s Long Day’s Journey into Night counts as his most impressive (and notorious) success.

films.title: There, There + A Soft Touch
films.synopsis: Halifax filmmaker Heather Young follows up her acclaimed feature debut Murmur (2019) with two equally assured and empathetic works of Maritime realism, the feature There, There and a new short, A Soft Touch.

films.title: Resurrection
films.synopsis: Resurrection is another fractured mirror from Bi Gan, with stunning feats of spectacle at each bookend, and curious shapeshifting tales in between.

films.title: Twin Peaks: The Return
films.synopsis: Cavernously strange, brilliantly nostalgia-averse, and staggering in its scope and singularity, Twin Peaks: The Return is an unshakable triumph of maximal film art.

films.title: Inland Empire
films.synopsis: A hallucinatory tour of a haunted-funhouse Hollywood, Lynch’s extra-outré Inland Empire stars Laura Dern as a past-prime actor offered the lead in a movie rumoured to be cursed.

films.title: Lost Highway
films.synopsis: Lost Highway is David Lynch’s terrifying, polarizing, Möbius-strip of a horror flick.

films.title: The Straight Story
films.synopsis: A pure distillation of David Lynch’s love for the sturdiness and uncanniness of people and things shaped by the American Midwest, The Straight Story is a film of single-minded purpose.

films.title: The Snow Queen
films.synopsis: Based on Hans Christian Andersen’s most ambitiously detailed fairy tale, The Snow Queen is a landmark in animated feature filmmaking.

films.title: A Better Tomorrow III: Love & Death in Saigon
films.synopsis: Tsui Hark took over the director’s chair for the final action-packed installment of the Better Tomorrow series, an origin story that precedes the first film’s events.

films.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me
films.synopsis: Fire Walk with Me, David Lynch’s feature-length prequel to his and Mark Frost’s seminal ’90s television series, must surely rank among the greatest critical about-faces in recent memory.

films.title: Mulholland Dr.
films.synopsis: David Lynch’s Mulholland Dr., a beguiling mix of Hollywood satire, film noir, and fever dream, is now regarded as one of the greatest movies of all time (eighth according to Sight and Sound’s latest decennial poll).

films.title: Bullet in the Head
films.synopsis: Bullet in the Head, John Woo’s wildest and weightiest action spectacle, is also his most personal movie, one that emerged out of his separation from producer Tsui Hark.

films.title: Wild at Heart
films.synopsis: “A picture about finding love in hell,” said the director, Wild at Heart prefigured the dial turn on sex and violence that would become de rigueur in the Tarantino age.

films.title: True Chronicles of the Blida Joinville Psychiatric Hospital in the Last Century, when Dr Frantz Fanon Was Head of the Fifth Ward between 1953 and 1956
films.synopsis: Frantz Fanon, one of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century, is most celebrated for his postcolonial philosophy. But Fanon was also a trained psychiatrist, an experience that deeply informed his worldview.

films.title: The Fullness of Time
films.synopsis: In The Fullness of Time, American artist Cauleen Smith repurposes the languages of physicists and astronomers to decode the rage, grief, elation, and hope that surround the post-Katrina reality of New Orleans.

films.title: The Elephant Man
films.synopsis: David Lynch’s surefooted, eight-time Oscar-nominated film The Elephant Man is a carefully crafted study of human dignity in the face of industrialized cruelty.

films.title: Dune
films.synopsis: The years have been forgiving to Dune: its grotesqueries, industrial-steampunk aesthetic, handcrafted effects, and introduction of Kyle MacLachlan now present as fascinating and at times stridently brilliant artifacts in the evolution of Lynch.

films.title: Six Shorts by David Lynch
films.synopsis: This program collects David Lynch’s formative early works, along with an eerie one-minute short from 1995 commissioned for the centenary of cinema.

films.title: Eraserhead
films.synopsis: David Lynch’s hair-hoisting first feature Eraserhead is one of those rare movies that genuinely seems to have spilled directly out of the unconscious mind—unmediated, unadulterated, and “carrying bits of brain tissue with it.”

films.title: Twin Peaks: The Pilot
films.synopsis: The TV show that redrew the boundaries of the medium, Twin Peaks became an overnight sensation on the brilliance of its broadcast pilot, seeding the premise that would ensnare viewers the world over: who killed Laura Palmer?

films.title: A Better Tomorrow II
films.synopsis: A Better Tomorrow II begins as a policier, with Kit (Leslie Cheung) going undercover only to run into his brother (Ti Lung), paroled and doing his own top-secret work in Hong Kong’s criminal underground.

films.title: Blue Velvet
films.synopsis: Blue Velvet, David Lynch’s great masterpiece of the 1980s, is perhaps the fulcrum of his career—the film that rehabilitated his name and endeared his dark, distinctive filmmaking to a generation of disturbed moviegoers.

films.title: Levers
films.synopsis: Following her award-winning Ste. Anne, Métis filmmaker Rhayne Vermette is back with her experimental sophomore feature Levers.

films.title: God’s Comedy
films.synopsis: God’s Comedy is a popular movie: its exacting, suspenseful portioning of perversity, normalcy, and voyeuristic beauty is displayed with total awareness of its audience’s likely repulsion or acceptance.

films.title: A Better Tomorrow
films.synopsis: John Woo’s influence as an on-set improviser of peerless action and male melodrama began with A Better Tomorrow.

films.title: Mother of Snow Cranes
films.synopsis: Mother of Snow Cranes is a portrait of one woman’s unwavering commitment to wildlife and a reflection on resilience, belonging, and connections across nature, humanity, and cultural borders.

films.title: Hovering Over the Water
films.synopsis: The serene beauty of the coast-hugging house where Laura Rossellini (Laura Morante) and her extended family gather in Hovering Over the Water contains, perhaps, everything you could ask of a movie.

films.title: The Last Dive
films.synopsis: The Last Dive is a film of immediate, freewheeling pain and pleasure, as well as mirage-like formal maneuvers coded with the early experimentation of silent cinema.

films.title: Recollections of the Yellow House
films.synopsis: In Recollections of the Yellow House, João César Monteiro appears as João de Deus, a tenant in a Lisbon apartment building who suffers from tramp-like indignities.

films.title: City on Fire
films.synopsis: One of the seminal works of the heroic bloodshed genre, Ringo Lam’s City on Fire, released just six months after John Woo’s blockbuster A Better Tomorrow, cemented Chow Yun-fat as an action superstar.

films.title: Trails
films.synopsis: As if rewriting a Portuguese epic from beginning to end, Trails braids the ancient (voiceover) and contemporary (performance) with ephemeral shifts in magic-hour light cast over centuries of ritual and stories within stories.

films.title: Silvestre
films.synopsis: In Silvestre all requires belief, or is not exactly as it seems.

films.title: Casanova’s Return
films.synopsis: Casanova’s Return centres on hugely successful, critically acclaimed filmmaker Leo Bernardi (played with endearing world-weary charm by Toni Servillo), who is approaching the end of his career but refusing to accept his slow decline.

films.title: Porcelain War
films.synopsis: Amidst the chaos and destruction of the Russian invasion, three Ukrainian artists defiantly find inspiration and beauty as they defend their culture and country in Porcelain War.

films.title: Whites Wash at Ninety
films.synopsis: Despite her young age, a woman faces a lifetime’s worth of personal upheaval in Whites Wash at Ninety.

films.title: Ciao Ciao
films.synopsis: In Ciao Ciao, what seemed like a harmless dinner party soon becomes an intense (and quite funny) evening as social graces crumble into chaos.

films.title: Beyond the Blue Border
films.synopsis: Based on a novel by athlete-turned-author Dorit Linke, writer-director Sarah Neumann’s feature debut Beyond the Blue Border is a gripping, youth-centred historical drama set during the final bulwark of authoritarianism in 1989 East Germany.

films.title: Even Pigs Go to Heaven
films.synopsis: Even Pigs Go to Heaven is a zany musical comedy about the dramas of villagers and their pigs in the Zagorje hills, narrated by Jesus Christ himself!

films.title: Brando with a Glass Eye
films.synopsis: Brando with a Glass Eye is a metatextual tale of a wannabe actor (Yiannis Niarros) who resorts to crime to finance a trip to New York’s famed Actors Studio.

films.title: We Treat Women Too Well
films.synopsis: Adapted from Raymond Queneau’s provocative novel, We Treat Women Too Well transposes the Oulipo author’s mix of political and sexual warfare from Ireland’s Easter Rising to the end of the Spanish Civil War.

films.title: Three Kilometres to the End of the World
films.synopsis: In Three Kilometres to the End of the World, a teenager’s secret relationship is discovered, setting off a chain of events that will shake his family and community to the core.

films.title: Franz
films.synopsis: Director Agnieszka Holland, as a follow-up to her acclaimed (and highly controversial) Green Border, has channelled a variety of impulses and formal approaches into her own version of Franz Kafka.

films.title: Maria’s Silence
films.synopsis: With Maria’s Silence, Dāvis Sīmanis Jr. offers a powerful historical drama shot in luminous black and white.

films.title: The Summer Book
films.synopsis: In The Summer Book, Glenn Close plays the sage and inquisitive grandmother, full of flinty appreciation for nature’s wisdom, to the film’s version of Sophia (Emily Matthews), a child who’s given the space to grow over a Nordic island summer.

films.title: Drowning Dry
films.synopsis: Laurynas Bareiša’s often startling family drama Drowning Dry, rendered in a fragmented narrative reminiscent of Atom Egoyan’s The Sweet Hereafter, is a powerful, affecting tale of two families whose summer holiday goes awry.

films.title: Omen
films.synopsis: A tour de force of magical realism from Belgian-Congolese rapper-turned-filmmaker Baloji Tshiani, Omen uses enigmatic storytelling and stunning imagery to tell the story of a Congolese man’s traumatic homecoming.

films.title: Dreamtown
films.synopsis: A rock star that never was is at the heart of Steven McKenna’s Dreamtown, a lived-in character study that draws inspiration from the orbit of the director’s musician father, a member of the Dublin band Shaine.

films.title: Wedlock
films.synopsis: Magdalena Ralcheva’s sumptuously filmed drama Wedlock stages intergenerational love triangles and long-simmering familial resentments in an unforgiving landscape.

films.title: The Land in the Shadows
films.synopsis: A tautly constructed, very impressive debut film from writer-director Lukas Grevis, In the Land of the Shadows tells the powerful, timely story of growing xenophobia in a village in Luxembourg.

films.title: Making Of
films.synopsis: Making Of, an entry by Cédric Kahn in the film-about-filmmaking genre, is a high-energy comedic gloss on the snares of industry work, starting with the compromises and hypocrisies of international co-production financing.

films.title: Memory Lane
films.synopsis: A beautiful, tender, and very funny love story, Memory Lane was the Dutch submission to the Academy Award for International Feature last year.

films.title: Smaragda: I Got Thick Skin and I Can’t Jump
films.synopsis: After being fired from her job as a children’s television host for having an inadequate social media following, eccentric Smaragda (Niovi Charalambous) moves into her recently deceased mother’s house and takes a job at a nearby resort.

films.title: Faith & John Hubley × 7
films.synopsis: As directors of independent animation, Faith and John Hubley pursued what Faith called “the directness and passion of a child’s vision.” This program includes seven films, including Windy Day, Moonbird, and Cockaboody.

films.title: I Accidentally Wrote a Book
films.synopsis: Hungarian writer-director Nóra Lakos returns to EUFF with I Accidentally Wrote a Book, a whimsical coming-of-age film about a young writer navigating girlhood and personal loss.

films.title: Breached
films.synopsis: Breached revolves around Maciek, a man whose life unravels following a car accident that claims the life of Janina,

films.title: Réveillon
films.synopsis: Michal Kunes Kováč’s sharply observed chamber drama Réveillon offers both biting humour and emotional depth as it examines the fragile bonds that hold people together.

films.title: Savanna and the Mountain
films.synopsis: Set in the rugged Portuguese countryside, Savanna and the Mountain follows the residents of Covas do Barroso, a small village confronted with the arrival of a massive lithium mining project.

films.title: Hammarskjöld
films.synopsis: In this sweeping biographical drama, director Per Fly follows Hammarskjöld’s journey from his rise in international politics to his untimely death in a mysterious plane crash in 1961.

films.title: Peacock
films.synopsis: Inspired by the same real-life agencies as in the buzzy Brendan Fraser feature Rental Family, Bernhard Wenger’s debut Peacock takes a decidedly more satirical approach in the vein of Ruben Östlund or Maren Ade.

films.title: 8 Views of Lake Biwa
films.synopsis: The winner of prizes for direction, acting, cinematography, and four other technical prizes at this year’s Estonian Film and Television Awards, Marko Raat’s fourth film 8 Views of Lake Biwa takes its premise and title from an unusual transposition.

films.title: The Land of Short Sentences
films.synopsis: Sofie Torp is the centre of The Land of Short Sentences, playing a woman who feels like every culture-clash joke and relationship conflict could be an incremental step in a plot to ruin her life.

films.title: Crocodile Tears
films.synopsis: In his much anticipated feature-length debut Crocodile Tears, Tumpal Tampulolon expands the motherhood horror subgenre, situating an Indonesian coming-of-age story in a disquieted atmosphere with supernatural overtones.

films.title: Laura Taler: the village trilogy + Matryoshka Crush
films.synopsis: Join Ottawa-based artist and filmmaker Laura Taler for a special 30th anniversary screening of her 1995 dancefilm classic the village trilogy accompanied by a genre-defying new work, Matryoshka Crush.

films.title: The Melt Goes On Forever: The Art & Times of David Hammons
films.synopsis: The Melt Goes On Forever chronicles the singular career of the elusive Black American art star David Hammons, from Watts rebellion-era ’60s L.A. to global art world prominence today.

films.title: The Asthenic Syndrome
films.synopsis: An avant-garde comedy and a strangely linked collection of tragic short stories, Kira Muratova’s The Asthenic Syndrome might be the final great work of the Soviet film industry.

films.title: Peking Opera Blues
films.synopsis: Perhaps no other film better displays the blink-and-you-miss-it inventiveness of Tsui Hark than Peking Opera Blues.

films.title: The Killer
films.synopsis: After director John Woo and producer Tsui Hark locked horns over A Better Tomorrow II, Woo drummed up his own financing to underwrite follow-up The Killer, his squib-soaked homage to Melville’s Le samouraï and a bona fide action classic.

films.title: #skoden
films.synopsis: Damien Eagle Bear shares the story of fellow Kainai First Nation member Pernell Bad Arm, the face of an infamous meme, the now-iconic slang word “skoden” meaning “let’s go, then.”

films.title: Eve and the Fire Horse
films.synopsis: Celebrating the 20th anniversary of a homegrown classic, this edition of “Cinema Thinks the World” welcomes director Julia Kwan to discuss her stylishly shot charmer Eve and the Fire Horse.

films.title: Ways of Seeing: short short films by Gordon Payne
films.synopsis: Gordon Payne’s three short films, made in the late 1960s and early ’70s, unfold as tactile meditations on vision, ritual, and the restless politics of seeing.

films.title: 13-Hour Horror Movie Marathon
films.synopsis: For 13 unholy hours, The Cinematheque will ply your senses (and fray your nerves) with a non-stop stream of scary movies, a curated grab bag of arthouse horror we think aficionados and neophytes alike will simply die for!

films.title: A Chinese Ghost Story II
films.synopsis: A Chinese Ghost Story II is a typical sequel: larger monsters, more supporting characters, and higher stakes. But this is also a film where Tsui Hark and Ching Siu-tung sharpen all the political subtext their action-horror-comedy film can muster.

films.title: A Chinese Ghost Story III
films.synopsis: Set a century after the first Chinese Ghost Story, this final entry in Tsui Hark and Ching Siu-tung’s series serves as a reboot, swapping Leslie Cheung’s tax collector for Fong, a monk-in-training played by a pre-stardom Tony Leung Chiu-wai.

films.title: The Eve of Ivan Kupalo
films.synopsis: Little seen outside of Ukraine even today, The Eve of Ivan Kupalo (1965), shot on 70mm, is the second film directed by Yurii Illienko, the most prominent contributor to Ukrainian poetic cinema.

films.title: The Lost Letter
films.synopsis: Borys Ivchenko’s adaptation of the Mykola Hohol story The Lost Letter is a comedic blend of road movie and adventure film, drawing deeply on Cossack legend, superstition, and folk mysticism.

films.title: Fremont
films.synopsis: Challenging American dream platitudes, Fremont presents an unsensational depiction of PTSD and survivor’s guilt that is complicated by longing for companionship and a less monotonous life.

films.title: World as Tripod
films.synopsis: In partnership with Centre A’s group exhibition featuring new work by Jaewoo Kang, Lauraine Mak, and Casey Wei, this omnibus screening takes that exhibition’s title, World as Tripod, as its thematic prompt.

films.title: Bride of Frankenstein
films.synopsis: While its title promises a marriage, Bride of Frankenstein opens with a rather serious problem: both Dr. Frankenstein (Colin Clive) and the Monster he created (Boris Karloff) are by all accounts dead. Not for long!

films.title: Red Beard
films.synopsis: Considered by some to be the concluding chapter of Kurosawa’s greatest period, Red Beard is, in any event, a watershed work for the master: his last picture in black-and-white, and his final collaboration with leading man Mifune Toshiro.

films.title: The Hidden Fortress
films.synopsis: Kurosawa was named Best Director at Berlin for The Hidden Fortress, a swashbuckling swords-and-samurai farce shot on the slopes of Mount Fuji and set in 16th-century Japan.

films.title: The Proposal
films.synopsis: American conceptual artist and writer Jill Magid, whose work featured in Griffin Art Projects’s TENXTEN exhibition, resumes her ongoing interrogation of power structures in The Proposal, described by the artist as “part thriller, part romance.”

films.title: SFMF Series 2
films.synopsis: Small File Media Festival 2025 continues with “The Red Room: Particles of Love and Resistance” and “Compagnons du devoir”

films.title: SFMF Series 3
films.synopsis: On Running and Hiding depicts two troubled gig-working microinfluencers struggling to make it in Vancouver. Join us for the world premiere of this commissioned feature-length small-file film by directors Liam and Will Riley

films.title: A Chinese Ghost Story
films.synopsis: A Chinese Ghost Story centres on the relationship between hapless tax collector Choi-san (Leslie Cheung) and an ethereal ghost (Joey Wong).

films.title: SFMF Series 1
films.synopsis: Small File Media Festival 2025 begins with “The Blue Room: Waves of Veiled Revelations” and “Sleep Spells”

films.title: Joonam
films.synopsis: A Sundance 2023 official selection, Joonam is a heartfelt and intimate documentary on identity, intergenerational trauma, and the search for belonging that resonates across diasporas.

films.title: Stories to Share: Respecting the Spirit
films.synopsis: “Stories to Share: Respecting the Spirit” highlights the importance of the stories of where we come from, in Cole Forrest’s Ozigwan (Tail of Serpent), Asia Youngman’s N'xaxaitkw, and Northlore, co-directed by Melaina Sheldon and David Hamelin.

films.title: Yojimbo
films.synopsis: Mifune Toshiro was named Best Actor at Venice for his spirited performance in Yojimbo, a jaw-droppingly kinetic jidaigeki (period drama) that has been called “Kurosawa’s most commanding film.”

films.title: Sanjuro
films.synopsis: Kurosawa lovingly spoofs the conventions of the samurai genre in the fast and funny Sanjuro, his made-on-the-quick (but highly regarded) sequel to the hugely popular Yojimbo.
Isiah Medina’s feature debut uses “88:88” as a marker of the nothing (no thing) of poverty and the possibility of counting life anew.

films.title: Linda Linda Linda
films.synopsis: What makes Yamashita Nobuhiro’s cult film Linda Linda Linda so uniquely memorable is how it captures the feeling of new friendship with such understated patience—a lot can happen in three days, even if it feels like nothing dramatic is building.

films.title: Tea Creek
films.synopsis: In Tea Creek, filmmaker Ryan Dickie follows Jacob Beaton and his team across a year of food sovereignty training, capturing the ups and downs of farm life, the healing of ongoing and historical wounds of colonization, and the uplifting of community

films.title: Perfumed Nightmare
films.synopsis: There is no better film to kick off a new season of “Cinema Thinks the World” than Perfumed Nightmare, Kidlat Tahimik’s landmark DIY satire of Western cultural and imperial dominance.

films.title: The Gold Rush
films.synopsis: Charlie Chaplin called The Gold Rush “the picture I want to be remembered by.” It is perhaps the masterpiece with more memorable, perfectly structured comic sequences than in any of his other films.

films.title: Russian Ark
films.synopsis: A miracle of the cinema, Alexander Sokurov’s astonishing Russian Ark is a single, spellbinding, time-travelling tracking shot—the longest uninterrupted Steadicam shot in cinema history—through Saint Petersburg’s famed Hermitage museum.

films.title: The Friends
films.synopsis: Set over the dog days of a summer vacation, The Friends chronicles—in exemplary crane-assisted, continuous-take Somai fashion—the friendship between three death-obsessed classmates and a neighbourhood recluse.

films.title: Hard Boiled
films.synopsis: The art of the action sequence arguably reached its zenith in John Woo’s unapologetically in-your-face heroic bloodshed epic Hard Boiled, an exclamation point on the balletic gunplay genre he helped define and deliver to the mainstream.

films.title: Song of the Exile
films.synopsis: Ann Hui’s most autobiographical film, Song of the Exile, is also her most formally refined, an emotionally layered portrait of familial conflict that counts as the closest connection between the Hong Kong and Taiwanese New Waves.

films.title: Climate in Therapy
films.synopsis: In Nathan Grossman’s Climate in Therapy, climate scientists sign up for a group therapy session facilitated by therapist Richard Beck. Beck employs Socratic questioning to unearth complicated feelings as the group discloses their emotional selves.

films.title: Boat People
films.synopsis: Hui’s ultimate and profoundly committed concern in Boat People isn’t only a political stance but a modernist inclination. What, the film asks, are the powers and the limitations of the camera?

films.title: Throne of Blood
films.synopsis: Kurosawa Akira’s Throne of Blood, a rousing, ravishing take on the Bard’s great tragedy, ranks as one of the director’s finest works, and one of cinema’s great Shakespeare adaptations. It was also said to be T.S. Eliot’s favourite film.

films.title: Ikiru
films.synopsis: Many Kurosawa admirers cite Ikiru, a deeply affecting piece of humanist cinema, as one of the great director’s pinnacle achievements—it was among Kurosawa’s own favourites.

films.title: La Commune (Paris, 1871)
films.synopsis: The experience of the La Commune (Paris 1871) is something overwhelming yet distilled, a sprawling portrait of “pre-revolutionary atmosphere” unfurled in the space of a warehouse among a cast of over 200 actors.

films.title: Nechako: It Will Be a Big River Again
films.synopsis: Lyana Patrick’s film Nechako captures the Stellat’en Nations’ resistance against industry, banding together to advocate for their people and fight to restore a river and a way of life.

films.title: His Motorbike, Her Island
films.synopsis: Likely Obayashi Nobuhiko’s most beloved movie, His Motorbike, Her Island is a branching narrative of love ending and beginning, in which desire is channelled through totems—photographs, songs, and beloved Kawasakis—that mix tenderness and danger.

films.title: Gina
films.synopsis: By turns a grindhouse rape-revenge picture and a leftist labour-abuse drama, Denys Arcand’s Gina is a strange but potent brew.

films.title: Monisme
films.synopsis: Artist and filmmaker Riar Rizaldi’s debut feature Monisme utilizes a myriad of aesthetic strategies to present a constantly morphing study of human nature under the pervasive threat of volcanic destruction.

films.title: Whisper of the Heart
films.synopsis: Whisper of the Heart, perhaps the most underrated Studio Ghibli film (it is directed neither by Miyazaki Hayao nor Takahata Isao), turns 30 this year.

films.title: The Island Closest to Heaven
films.synopsis: Director Obayashi Nobuhiko and idol Harada Tomoyo reteamed for The Island Closest to Heaven, a minor-key voyage in which sunny tourism provides cover for the harder-to-define process of discovery in the wake of loss.

films.title: 7 Walks with Mark Brown
films.synopsis: Pierre Creton’s superb documentary follow-up to A Prince, 7 Walks with Mark Brown, eschews the weirdness and sex, but much remains familiar in this edifying tour of Normandy’s indigenous flora.

films.title: By the Stream
films.synopsis: By the Stream, the second Hong installment of 2024, reunites regulars Kim Minhee and Kwon Haehyo for an autumnal comic tale set on a liberal arts campus in Seoul.

films.title: School in the Crosshairs
films.synopsis: A popular idol film and an artistic cri de coeur all in one, Obayashi Nobuhiko’s School in the Crosshairs posits education—like only he could—as a societal microcosm, a magical space-to-earth portal, and a melodramatic bildungsroman.

films.title: The Girl Who Leapt Through Time
films.synopsis: Few films by Obayashi Nobuhiko have greater name recognition than his adaptation of Tsutsui Yasutaka’s The Girl Who Leapt Through Time, the first to make it to the screen.

films.title: Sweet Smell of Success
films.synopsis: Noir goes Broadway—and gets real nasty—in the sensationally scabrous Sweet Smell of Success, directed with impressive panache by Ealing comedy expert Alexander Mackendrick in his American debut.

films.title: High and Low
films.synopsis: Kurosawa Akira’s extraordinary High and Low, remixed this year in Spike Lee’s newest joint, is a morally complex thriller adapted from a novel by American crime writer Ed McBain.

films.title: Blast of Silence
films.synopsis: Be it a straggler of the classic noir cycle or a forerunner of the neo-noir next generation, either way you slice it, Allen Baron’s 1961 cult classic Blast of Silence is noir to the bone!

films.title: Réjeanne Padovani
films.synopsis: Quebec’s political class is brutally skewered—and real-life counterparts only negligibly veiled—in Denys Arcand’s expertly orchestrated crime-syndicate satire Réjeanne Padovani, a Directors’ Fortnight selection at Cannes.

films.title: Parvaneh
films.synopsis: Bahman Kiarostami’s intimate documentary offers an in-depth exploration of the life and artistic legacy of Parvaneh Etemadi, a pioneering Iranian visual artist.

films.title: Kiss of Death
films.synopsis: Henry Hathaway’s New York-shot noir Kiss of Death is famed for iconic tough guy Richard Widmark’s blistering (and Oscar-nominated) screen debut.

films.title: I, the Jury
films.synopsis: I, the Jury, the screen debut of Mickey Spillane’s bloodthirsty PI Mike Hammer, just happens to also be one of the niche films noir produced in 3D!

films.title: Pink Narcissus
films.synopsis: In 1963—the year of Scorpio Rising, Flaming Creatures, and Warhol’s first films—physique photographer James Bidgood began working on an ambitious homoerotic film fantasy he called Pink Narcissus.

films.title: Moonrise
films.synopsis: Frank Borzage, anointed Best Director at the very first Academy Awards in 1929, capstoned his distinguished career with Moonrise, an oneiric adaptation of Theodore Strauss’s serialized novel about an anguished psyche driven to violence.

films.title: Phantom Lady
films.synopsis: Carol “Kansas” Richman (Ella Raines) combs New York City’s shadowy streets and seedy bars in a race against time in Robert Siodmak’s suspenseful, stylish Phantom Lady, shot in the moody expressionist manner.

films.title: Double Indemnity
films.synopsis: Billy Wilder’s sordid, superlative Double Indemnity is one of film noir’s defining works.

films.title: Stray Dog
films.synopsis: Kurosawa Akira’s sizzling noir thriller Stray Dog offers an arrestingly atmospheric (and hellish) portrait of postwar Tokyo.

films.title: Dirty Money
films.synopsis: Dirty Money, the savagely bleak commercial debut of former NFB filmmaker Denys Arcand, is a sinuous tale of rapacity run amok, set firmly within the misanthropic gutter-world of film noir.

films.title: Crime Wave
films.synopsis: A taut, compelling, no-nonsense LA thriller from Hungarian émigré Andre de Toth (House of Wax), Crime Wave is one of the keystones of 1950s noir.

films.title: Peking Opera Blues
films.synopsis: Tsui Hark: Everything Is Unreal. Perhaps no other film better displays the blink-and-you-miss-it inventiveness of Tsui Hark than Peking Opera Blues.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in China II
films.synopsis: Because of the fact Once Upon a Time in China II was produced in only two months, it’s possessed by an energetic decisiveness possible only within the lightning-in-a-bottle Hong Kong film industry of the 1990s.

films.title: Vuntut Gwitchin—A Journey of Determination and Resilience
films.synopsis: In partnership with grunt gallery and Our World, a media-arts organization serving Indigenous communities, this month’s program shares the filmmaking journey of Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation.

films.title: Small Soldiers
films.synopsis: Joe Dante’s sincerely satirical and action-packed mini-blockbuster Small Soldiers posits the plain and uncomfortable truth that toy manufacturers and war profiteers are often in the same business: developing new ways to keep kids hooked.

films.title: To the Ends of the Earth
films.synopsis: Kurosawa Kiyoshi has multiple masterpieces that wander far outside the horror genre. There’s a strong case for To the Ends of the Earth, which stars former AKB48 member Maeda Atsuko, as the latest example of this kind of major achievement.

films.title: The Blade
films.synopsis: Tsui Hark’s genre revisionism was never more extreme than in The Blade, a raw stylistic departure that takes Chang Cheh’s The One-Armed Swordsman as its mythic departure point.

films.title: Cloud
films.synopsis: Kurosawa Kiyoshi’s latest feature Cloud is a human-scaled action film that shares some DNA with two films from the director’s past: Pulse and Serpent’s Path.

films.title: Creepy
films.synopsis: In Creepy, Kurosawa Kiyoshi, returning to the genre premise of his breakout film Cure, never flinches, injecting the dark lucidity of a fairy tale into a film otherwise led by pure suggestion.

films.title: Planes, Frames, and Autostereoscopy—3D Shorts
films.synopsis: The short films in this program by Peter Rose, Paul Sharits, Lillian F. Schwartz, Ken Jacobs, Makino Takashi, and Jodie Mack each take an unorthodox approach in their explorations of stereoscopic illusion.

films.title: Ulysses in the Subway + Around Is Around
films.synopsis: Ulysses in the Subway, a collaboration between experimental 3D maven Ken Jacobs, his wife Flo Jacobs, and Paul Kaiser and Marc Downie, tasks the image with describing what we hear. Preceded by Norman McLaren and Evelyn Lambart’s Around Is Around.

films.title: The Chinese Feast
films.synopsis: In The Chinese Feast, Tsui Hark finds an exact metaphor for his craft: the highly specialized, exactingly overseen world of high-end Chinese cuisine and its esoteric knowledge, to-the-limit labour, and awareness of an audience’s taste.

films.title: Korakrit Arunanondchai
films.synopsis: DIM Cinema presents a triple bill of works by Thai artist Korakrit Arunanondchai. These films, made between 2018 and 2021, highlight narratives around phenomenology, centralizing non-human animals, ghosts, and shadow histories.

films.title: Happy Hour
films.synopsis: A powerful affirmation of the immersive potential of cinema, Happy Hour is a slow-burning epic chronicling the emotional journey of four thirtysomething women in the misty seaside city of Kobe, Japan.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in China
films.synopsis: Tsui Hark’s mix of politics and action theatrics reached new levels of prestige in Once Upon a Time in China, in which anti-imperialist motivation and national pageantry are embodied in the glorious kung fu of Wong Fei-hung (Jet Li).

films.title: Working Class
films.synopsis: The tension between pure invention and reliable formula is expressed in the smooth plot mechanics of Working Class, an early workplace comedy from director (and featured co-star) Tsui Hark.

films.title: Shanghai Blues
films.synopsis: A synthesis of both his provocative streak and his desire to capture Hong Kong’s popular imagination, Shanghai Blues, a personal favourite of director Tsui Hark, is the first film produced under the independent banner of his Film Workshop.

films.title: Green Snake
films.synopsis: A film full of mythic beasts, practical special effects, and colour filters, Green Snake is also a masterful allegory on the process of becoming human: contradictory desires, arbitrary ethics, and cycles of death and life.

films.title: Unsettled Reality, Tomorrow’s Imagination
films.synopsis: This selection of five films by young Iranian women filmmakers delves into the fragile and shifting nature of contemporary existence as seen through their unique perspectives.

films.title: Dogtooth
films.synopsis: With shades of Haneke-like distantiation and Noé-esque extremity, Dogtooth stages a deranged, domestic nightmare within the suburban home of a Greek nuclear family.

films.title: Invention
films.synopsis: Tantalizing mysteries abound in Courtney Stephens’s Invention, a fiction feature by the experimental documentarian that nearly calls for scare quotes to stress the nonfictional undercurrents at work.

films.title: Barry Lyndon
films.synopsis: Barry Lyndon, Stanley Kubrick’s lavish adaptation of Thackeray’s picaresque novel, has since made a long, inexorable climb to masterpiece status—and to recognition as one of Kubrick’s pinnacle achievements.

films.title: We Have Many Names + Vincent the Dutchman
films.synopsis: In the 1970s, Zetterling channelled her energy into television; two rarely screened works from her time in that medium are presented here: Vincent the Dutchman and We Have Many Names.

films.title: The Rubber Gun
films.synopsis: Allan Moyle’s quasi-autobiographical debut feature The Rubber Gun is a loose, largely improvised vérité portrait of a makeshift family of peddlers and addicts in the atrophying counterculture of ’70s Montreal.

films.title: Keepers of the Fire + Our Grandmother the Inlet
films.synopsis: Keepers of the Fire, directed by Christine Welsh, follows Indigenous women storytellers, dreamers, healers, and fighters across Canada. Preceded by Our Grandmother the Inlet, which follows Kayah George and her grandmother Ta7a.

films.title: I Am Ishi: The Performance Art Film
films.synopsis: Directed by acclaimed visual artist Dana Claxton and produced by the Ishi Collective, I Am Ishi, an uncanny, experimental feature-length work, serves as both document and expansion of a performance piece by James Luna.

films.title: Night Games
films.synopsis: Zetterling’s second feature Night Games, set in a memory-haunted mansion that wields a soul-crushing, incapacitating power over its occupants, was deemed such an extreme provocation that its Venice premiere screenings were removed from public view.

films.title: Four Nights of a Dreamer
films.synopsis: The rarely screened Four Nights of a Dreamer is Robert Bresson’s great forgotten masterpiece, a stark yet haunting ode to romantic idealism and the capriciousness of love.

films.title: The Sealed Soil
films.synopsis: The Sealed Soil, the earliest surviving feature directed by an Iranian woman, follows a marriage-scorning Iranian teenager (Flora Shabaviz) whose seething indignation over her state of bondage is presumed to be the work of demonic possession.

films.title: Inedia
films.synopsis: Set on a Gulf Island in British Columbia, Inedia creates, with glistening 16mm cinematography, a trance-like atmosphere mirroring protagonist Cora’s fragile mental state.

films.title: Lancelot du Lac
films.synopsis: Long a pet Bresson project (he had been trying for two decades to bring it to the screen), Lancelot du Lac presents a masterfully anti-heroic portrait of Camelot and the Age of Chivalry in decline.

films.title: Amorosa
films.synopsis: For her final feature Amorosa, Mai Zetterling realized a full-circle project: a biopic of Agnes von Krusenstjerna (Stina Ekblad), the author Zetterling adapted in her first feature Loving Couples.

films.title: Fyodor Khitruk and Yuri Norstein × 6
films.synopsis: This program combines some of the best all-ages animated work from directors Fyodor Khitruk and Yuri Norstein while at the Russian studio Soyuzmultfilm, including Khitruk’s Winnie-the-Pooh and Norstein’s Hedgehog in the Fog.

films.title: Doktor Glas
films.synopsis: Adapted from Hjalmar Söderberg’s acclaimed novel, Doktor Glas is Mai Zetterling’s most stylistically controlled film and a subtle masterwork about the neuroses of domineering men.

films.title: The Girls
films.synopsis: Rejected by Swedish audiences and critics, The Girls shows Mai Zetterling at the height of her provocative powers.

films.title: Loving Couples
films.synopsis: Mai Zetterling’s feature debut Loving Couples is so ambitiously crafted that it earned comparison to Citizen Kane upon its release.

films.title: Lily Jue Sheng: very abstract and a little bit harsh
films.synopsis: “very abstract and a little bit harsh” traces the political consciousness of Lily Jue Sheng’s practice through a decade of their experience around unstable cinema economies.

films.title: Une femme douce
films.synopsis: Based on a story by Dostoevsky, Une femme douce is Bresson’s first film in colour and marks the stand-out screen debut of Dominique Sanda, a Bresson non-pro who went on to have a major career in European cinema.

films.title: Mouchette
films.synopsis: A work of extraordinary purity and grace, the almost unbearably moving Mouchette serves as something of a companion piece to Au hasard Balthazar in its somber but ultimately profound account of earthly suffering.

films.title: The Ballad of Suzanne Césaire
films.synopsis: Visual artist Madeleine Hunt-Ehrlich’s first feature film The Ballad of Suzanne Césaire isolates a gap in the history of the Surrealists, interrogating a void of information through revisionist restaging.

films.title: Compensation
films.synopsis: Zeinabu irene Davis’s Compensation contains two timelines, two pairs of lovers, two cinematic styles. It’s also the first film to draw on, without tokenizing, Black Deaf culture, and maybe the most graceful silent-film homage in all of cinema.

films.title: The Marriage Project
films.synopsis: Directed by Atieh Attarzadeh and Hesam Eslami, The Marriage Project offers a close look into the world of individuals dealing with mental health challenges while pursuing one of the most universal human experiences—love.

films.title: India Song
