films.synopsis: Marguerite Duras’s most celebrated work India Song is a mesmerizing, almost incantatory experience with few stylistic precedents in the history of cinema.

films.title: L’argent
films.synopsis: L’argent, the awe-inspiring farewell film from one of cinema’s most rigorous masters, shared the Best Director prize at Cannes in 1983 and was selected by many critics and filmmakers as one of the greatest films of the 1980s.

films.title: Ran
films.synopsis: The magisterial Ran, the great masterpiece of Kurosawa’s late period, is both an enthralling piece of epic moviemaking and an inspired reworking of King Lear.

films.title: The Trial of Joan of Arc
films.synopsis: Bresson’s startling, searing take on one of cinema’s most filmed stories is based, on the actual transcripts of Joan of Arc’s trial—here distilled into the very essence of the spare Bressonian aesthetic.

films.title: Au hasard Balthazar
films.synopsis: Described by Godard as “the world in an hour and a half” and by Michael Haneke as “the most precious of all cinematic jewels,” Au hasard Balthazar is one of Bresson’s most deeply affecting, lyrical, and accessible works.

films.title: Night Raiders
films.synopsis: Night Raiders, directed by Danis Goulet, takes place in 2043, a dystopian post-war future, and stars Elle-Máijá Tailfeathers and Brooklyn Letexier-Hart as Cree mother and daughter Niska and Waseese.

films.title: Fading Landscapes: The Films of Linda Fenstermaker
films.synopsis: In the work of Washington-based filmmaker, curator, and farmer Linda Fenstermaker, we soon recognize in this collection of impressionistic 16mm films that quiet connection is the point—and that the slow persistence of nature is subtly revolutionary.

films.title: Pickpocket
films.synopsis: Pickpocket definitely ranks as a chef d’oeuvre: Richard Roud called it Bresson’s masterpiece, Cahiers du cinéma voted it the greatest French film of the postwar era, and its influence can be traced across many a disciple’s career.

films.title: Les dames du Bois de Boulogne
films.synopsis: Bresson’s second feature Les dames du Bois de Boulogne is “a landmark in cinema history … Its influence on subsequent French cinema is far from exhausted” (David Thomson).

films.title: The Saragossa Manuscript
films.synopsis: The Saragossa Manuscript is a hallucinatory epic adapted from Polish nobleman Jan Potocki’s 19th-century novel, which relates the adventures of one Alphonse van Worden, a Belgian officer making his way across Spain during the Napoleonic Wars.

films.title: Memoir of a Snail
films.synopsis: Recently nominated for an Academy Award for Best Animated Feature, Adam Elliot’s tragicomedy Memoir of a Snail recounts the misfortunes of a misfit named Grace who becomes obsessed with snails.

films.title: Nadia: The Secret of Blue Water × 4
films.synopsis: Before Neon Genesis Evangelion, Anno Hideaki was tasked with directing the series Nadia: The Secret of Blue Water, a Paris-set adventure tale that branches out into hidden worlds.

films.title: Batang West Side
films.synopsis: Batang West Side, Lav Diaz’s riveting and, until now, scarcely seen fourth feature, is a monumental police procedural set in winter-scourged Jersey City.

films.title: Les anges du péché
films.synopsis: Bresson’s first feature Les anges du péché is more elegant and stylized than the sparer masterworks to come but nonetheless boldly announces the central Bressonian themes: isolation, suffering, martyrdom, and the struggle for redemption and grace.

films.title: Diary of a Country Priest
films.synopsis: Perhaps the quintessential Bresson picture, Diary of a Country Priest is the work that established the aesthetic austerity and metaphysical intensity of the director’s celebrated style.

films.title: The Devil, Probably
films.synopsis: Bresson’s penultimate film was his most controversial: The Devil, Probably was prohibited in France to viewers under 18 on the grounds it might incite suicide, and ignited a jury revolt at the Berlinale over its denial of the festival’s top prize.

films.title: A Man Escaped
films.synopsis: Hailed by François Truffaut at the time of its release as “the most important French film of the past ten years,” the masterly A Man Escaped is cited by many as Bresson’s pinnacle achievement.

films.title: Blue Sun Palace
films.synopsis: Blue Sun Palace, the debut feature of Chinese American writer-director Constance Tsang, delivers a delicately wrought portrait of working-class sisterhood in New York’s immigrant enclave of Flushing Chinatown.

films.title: Killer of Sheep
films.synopsis: Charles Burnett’s Killer of Sheep relates, with palpable tenderness and neorealist form, the struggles of a Black slaughterhouse worker raising a family in Watts, LA.

films.title: The End of Evangelion
films.synopsis: The End of Evangelion is creator Anno Hideaki’s spectacular—and spectacularly bleak—reimagining of Neon Genesis Evangelion’s polarizing ending, which stoked the ire of Eva stans when it aired in 1996.

films.title: Love & Pop
films.synopsis: Anime icon Anno Hideaki followed up his big-screen Neon Genesis Evangelion do-over (well, before the Rebuild retcon anyway) with Love & Pop, his first foray outside of animation.

films.title: Domestic Violence
films.synopsis: The Spring, the largest domestic violence shelter in Tampa, Florida, is the vantage point for Frederick Wiseman’s portrait of the aftermath of violent abuse.

films.title: Lindsay McIntyre: the space between the margins
films.synopsis: This program of experimental shorts by Lindsay McIntyre explores what was left behind by her great-grandmother Kumaa’naaq, and what is missing.

films.title: Hospital
films.synopsis: Set in the bustling Metropolitan Hospital in East Harlem, Hospital documents numerous emergency encounters of patients suffering from a range of medical and psychological ailments.

films.title: The Passenger
films.synopsis: Antonioni’s The Passenger, which marks a half-century in 2025, is both a central work of studio-cradled auteur cinema of the 1970s and a key film in the maestro’s oeuvre.

films.title: Zoo
films.synopsis: Zoo might be the high point of Wiseman’s development as a sound and image collagist, serving as the culmination of his animal films and containing within it multiple “types” of films.

films.title: Modern Times
films.synopsis: Charlie Chaplin’s final silent-esque comedy is a satire of working life—with a smile, or something like it.

films.title: The Store
films.synopsis: A luxurious companion film to Model, The Store relocates the concept of attraction from powerful images to the fine art of the coercive sales pitch.

films.title: Los olvidados
films.synopsis: Buñuel returned to the pantheon of international cinema with Los olvidados, his first masterpiece made as an exile in Mexico.

films.title: Él
films.synopsis: Male paranoia reaches pathological heights in Luis Buñuel’s Él, one of the emblematic films of the director’s prolific—and still largely neglected—Mexican period.

films.title: Belfast, Maine
films.synopsis: Frederick Wiseman’s artistic project is cumulative; each film builds upon the others, a progression that reaches its possible apotheosis in Belfast, Maine.

films.title: Holly Márie Parnell: Cabbage and hole in the stone
films.synopsis: Working in film and expanded cinema, the Irish/Canadian artist Holly Márie Parnell is drawn to the way power embeds and affects people and places.

films.title: Between Salt and Sweet Water
films.synopsis: Selected for the inaugural edition of the Directors’ Fortnight at Cannes, Between Salt and Sweet Water bears all the formal hallmarks of Michel Brault’s pioneering work in documentary, albeit recalibrated here to serve a fictional drama.

films.title: Les ordres
films.synopsis: Michel Brault’s second fiction feature Les ordres, a Kafkaesque political drama set during a dark chapter of our modern history, is one of Canadian cinema’s great works.

films.title: Little, Big, and Far
films.synopsis: A spiritual cousin to his 2012 masterpiece Museum Hours, Jem Cohen’s latest film Little, Big, and Far is at once an engrossing guided tour across the expanding universe and a ruminative portrait of an astronomer at an existential crossroads.

films.title: Leila and the Wolves
films.synopsis: Lebanese writer-director Heiny Srour’s sole foray into non-documentary is a paean to the lineage of Arab women who took up arms in the struggle for Palestinian liberation.

films.title: Gracefully
films.synopsis: Arash Eshaghi’s award-winning documentary Gracefully follows the life of an unnamed 80-year-old Iranian man who defies social norms through his passion for dancing in women’s clothing.

films.title: Fancy Dance
films.synopsis: This program pairs Idle No More by Anishinaabe director Ginger Cote, a member of Kitigan Zibi Anishinaabek First Nation, with Fancy Dance, directed by Seneca-Cayuga filmmaker Erica Tremblay.

films.title: Juvenile Court
films.synopsis: The longest and most dramatic of the director’s films to this point in his career, Juvenile Court is a film about close reading, of both adults who wield life-changing power and the young offenders that enter into their purview.

films.title: Titicut Follies
films.synopsis: Frederick Wiseman’s landmark debut Titicut Follies remains one of his most powerful, controversial, and influential works.

films.title: Model
films.synopsis: Frederick Wiseman’s ability to track the minutest shifts in behaviour, both rehearsed and “natural,” meets one of its greatest subjects in New York’s Zoli Agency, the starting point for Model.

films.title: Việt and Nam
films.synopsis: Vietnamese history is excavated, literally and figuratively, in director Trương Minh Quý’s astonishing new work Việt and Nam, a love story staged in subterranean corridors and haunted by spectres of unreconciled war.

films.title: Topos
films.synopsis: Antoinetta Angelidi is one of the most important Greek modernists, who has forged her own practice of feminist experimental cinema. In Topos, she constructs personal dreamscapes of masterly tableaux.

films.title: An Unfinished Film
films.synopsis: The latest feature from Lou Ye is a poignant time capsule of China’s first surge of COVID-19, rendered in a sly, evasive formal approach befitting the surreality of the lived-through experience.

films.title: Suzhou River
films.synopsis: Lou Ye, among the most controversial of the cohort, achieved acclaim abroad—and a two-year ban from filmmaking at home—for his sophomore feature Suzhou River, suppressed in China after its unauthorized Rotterdam premiere.

films.title: Country Mouse City Mouse Hamster
films.synopsis: Co-presented with the Morris and Helen Belkin Art Gallery and Or Gallery, this screening brings together works by visual artists Maggie Groat, Joan Jonas, Tiziana La Melia, and Krista Belle Stewart.

This intimate documentary portrait of poet Nanos Valaoritis and his wife, American surrealist artist and writer Marie Wilson, in the last years of their lives reveals a secret world full of wisdom and feeling.

films.title: High School II
films.synopsis: Whereas High School captured an education model where the predictability of outcomes is valorized, High School II counters with an open-ended one, with all the trials that emerge when student autonomy is foregrounded.

films.title: Welfare
films.synopsis: Often cited as the masterwork of Frederick Wiseman’s early period, Welfare stands as the director’s purest feat of structural precision, a closed loop that might contain a single day, or all the bureaucratic-inflicted suffering in the world.

films.title: The Travelling Players
films.synopsis: Described as “possibly the greatest film in the history of Greek cinema” (James Quandt, TIFF), Theo Angelopoulos’s stunning chef d’oeuvre The Travelling Players takes a panoramic look at Greek history during the turbulent years of 1939 to 1952.

films.title: Witches
films.synopsis: Grounded in her own story of being admitted to a psychiatric ward within a month of giving birth to her son, Witches writer-director Elizabeth Sankey shares her deeply personal experience of postpartum depression.

films.title: A Touch of Spice
films.synopsis: In A Touch of Spice, Fanis Iakovidis (George Corraface), a successful professor of astrophysics in Athens, takes a trip down memory lane through everything he knows about spices and the art of cooking.

films.title: Co Hoedeman × 5
films.synopsis: Five films by Co Hoedeman, one of the legendary animators that built the reputation of the National Film Board, screen in this program, including his Academy Award-winning The Sand Castle.

films.title: The Shepherds of Calamity
films.synopsis: Confidently directed by underappreciated Ethiopian Greek auteur Nikos Papatakis, whose work and sensibilities influenced the Greek Weird Wave, The Shepherds of Calamity is an original, sharp, and gutsy masterwork.

films.title: On the Waterfront
films.synopsis: Directed by the legendary Elia Kazan (America, America), On the Waterfront is an immensely satisfying and inspirational work and one of the finest of American films—a masterpiece of poetic realism.

films.title: The Empire
films.synopsis: In The Empire, Bruno Dumont’s new picture, the sci-fi goes intergalactic—and the CGI measures up—in a blockbuster-scaled parody about two warring races, Zeros and Ones, converging in northern France over the fate of an evil-incarnate baby.

films.title: La vie de Jésus
films.synopsis: Hardly the religious biopic promised by its title, La vie de Jésus is instead a dispassionate, austerely beautiful portrait of unemployed, uneducated, and unsympathetic youth in a small northern French town.

films.title: Boy on a Dolphin
films.synopsis: Boy on a Dolphin, the first Hollywood film to be shot on location in Greece, specifically the island of Hydra, is an adventure romance that is pure pleasure from first to last frame.

films.title: Essene
films.synopsis: The first and most contained of Frederick Wiseman’s community films, Essene is set at an Anglican monastery, a place where people search for serenity, complain about petty conflicts, and yearn for transcendence beyond material existence.

films.title: Blind
films.synopsis: In this first of four films Wiseman made at the Alabama Institute for the Deaf and Blind, the director sensitively connects two extremes—blind experience and visual images—to show without overcompensation the school’s rigidity and real achievements.

films.title: High School
films.synopsis: Education, first covered in High School, is arguably the central subject of Frederick Wiseman’s project: how an individual, a community, or, in his case, a filmmaker learns how to conceive of the world in which they live.

films.title: Central Park
films.synopsis: Frederick Wiseman’s cinema reached new heights in the 1990s, beginning with Central Park, an expansive vision of that rare thing in any city: common space both for and beyond human use.

films.title: Ninan Auassat: We, the Children
films.synopsis: Abenaki director Kim O’Bomsawin’s new documentary Ninan Auassat: We, the Children is a groundbreaking exploration of the new generation of Indigenous youth.

films.title: Welcome to the Dollhouse
films.synopsis: Todd Solondz’s breakthrough comedy Welcome to the Dollhouse, a benchmark of ’90s indie Americana, is a provocatively sanguine (and un-PC) portrait of preadolescent humiliation, with a perfectly cast Matarazzo as a winningly awkward “loser.”

films.title: The Burmese Harp
films.synopsis: Adapted from Takeyama Michio’s classic novel, and renowned for legendary composer Ifukube Akira’s haunting score, Ichikawa Kon’s The Burmese Harp is an epic humanist masterpiece.

films.title: Shoeshine
films.synopsis: One of the greatest achievements in the cinematic revolution known as Italian neorealism, Vittorio De Sica’s Shoeshine stands as a timeless masterpiece of trenchant social observation and stirring emotional humanism.

films.title: Picnic at Hanging Rock
films.synopsis: Peter Weir’s dreamy, mystical Picnic at Hanging Rock, one of the breakout works of the Australian New Wave, adapts Joan Lindsay’s acclaimed 1967 novel.

films.title: Experimental Shorts: 1974–1977
films.synopsis: Filmed between 1974 and 1977, these structuralist shorts present Bette Gordon’s emerging thematic preoccupations—sexuality, mobility, liminality, and the American national character—as well as her collaborations with filmmaker James Benning.

films.title: Variety
films.synopsis: In Bette Gordon’s landmark feminist noir Variety, Christine (Sandy McLeod), a sexually repressed aspiring writer, happens upon a front-of-house ticketing gig at Variety Photoplays, a historic porn theatre in New York City.

films.title: Agent of Happiness
films.synopsis: Agent of Happiness offers a first-person glimpse into the Kingdom of Bhutan’s Gross National Happiness index through the eyes of Amber, a government data collector.

films.title: Yokohama BJ Blues
films.synopsis: “My heart gets a rush / that I know is false,” sings BJ, the protagonist of Kudo Eiichi’s moody crime film Yokohama BJ Blues, a wounded romantic cult classic now receiving its first-ever North American release.

films.title: Viktor Kubal: Inventor of Slovak Animation
films.synopsis: On the heels of his centenary, the films of Viktor Kubal (1923–1997) are available in North America for the first time.

films.title: Winter Kept Us Warm
films.synopsis: An underseen landmark in Canadian cinema, David Secter’s debut film Winter Kept Us Warm is a quietly confident drama of desire and its power to redraw the lines between people and their assumed identities.

films.title: Drylongso
films.synopsis: Inspired by LA Rebellion filmmakers like Julie Dash and Zeinabu irene Davis, Cauleen Smith spent the summer of ’95 shooting her first and only narrative feature Drylongso, a film about sisterhood, grief, and the tools of artistic expression.

films.title: Il grido
films.synopsis: Before L’avventura, Michelangelo Antonioni made Il grido, a film many consider to be the most pivotal of his career, which charts the life-changing comedown after the end of a longterm relationship.

films.title: Silent House
films.synopsis: In Silent House, a childhood home remains a central character until the end, illustrating how every house’s story reflects the eras it has withstood. The film is a beautiful testament to the idea that a home can mirror the passage of time.

films.title: Broken Angel
films.synopsis: In tribute to the Annual Women’s Memorial March on February 14, we present Broken Angel, the powerful debut feature of InNiNeWak filmmaker Jules Arita Koostachin (Attawapiskat First Nation).

films.title: Youth (Homecoming)
films.synopsis: At once a culmination and a jumping-on point, Homecoming, the third and final volume in Wang Bing’s astonishing Youth saga, adopts an overhead view of the garment-industry apparatus while honing in on the lives of a few of its young characters.

films.title: Before Midnight
films.synopsis: In Before Midnight, it’s possible to see shades of all three films come out in Celine and Jesse’s probing, playful, and devastating talk, which searches for that most difficult thing—reinvention using a shared language.

films.title: Before Sunrise
films.synopsis: In Richard Linklater’s Before Sunrise, Celine (Julie Delpy) and Jesse (Ethan Hawke) meet as strangers on a European train; for the next 24 hours their attention is unbroken as they walk everywhere and discuss anything.

films.title: Before Sunset
films.synopsis: Tonally pitched between the daydream of a what-if fantasy and the dread of unrecoverable time, Celine and Jesse’s reunion in Before Sunset is one where incongruities combine with moments both conceptually thrilling and emotionally sublime.

films.title: Sorcerer
films.synopsis: Sorcerer, a remake of Clouzot’s The Wages of Fear, is today best appreciated as a Herzog-rivalling feat of production realism.

films.title: Café Daughter
films.synopsis: A Cree-Chinese girl struggles to keep her dreams from being extinguished in the affecting period drama Café Daughter, directed by Kanyen’kehá:ka (Mohawk) multimedia artist Shelley Niro, who is subject of a retrospective currently on view at the VAG.

films.title: The Wages of Fear
films.synopsis: Henri-Georges Clouzot (Les diaboliques) had his greatest success with the legendary thriller The Wages of Fear, which won best film honours at both Berlin and Cannes.

films.title: In Another Country
films.synopsis: Hong Sangsoo and Isabelle Huppert swiftly parlayed an informal dinner meeting into a film project titled In Another Country, with the legendary French star playing three versions of a woman named Anne.

films.title: Floating Life
films.synopsis: A hopscotching tale of multigenerational Chinese diaspora, the culture-shock comedy Floating Life concerns an aging Hong Kong couple who move to the Australian suburbs to live with their youngest daughter, a wound-up, assimilated career woman.

films.title: Drifting Petals
films.synopsis: Shot over five years and self-funded by wife-husband duo Clara Law and Eddie Fong, Drifting Petals, slipping amorphously between fiction and nonfiction registers, is a ghostly meditation on histories personal and political.

films.title: A Traveler’s Needs
films.synopsis: A Traveler’s Needs, the third collaboration between Hong Sangsoo and Isabelle Huppert, marks a high point in their creative partnership and yet another essential entry in the director’s chronicles of existential life.

films.title: Mermaid Legend
films.synopsis: Ikeda Toshiharu’s ’80s cult masterpiece Mermaid Legend is an exploitation film about the exploitation of women, labour, and nature, and a revenge thriller whose scale of cathartic fury is like nothing else in the genre.

films.title: Olivia Marie Golosky: nistawâyâw and Beyond
films.synopsis: This program traces the filmmaking journey of Two-Spirit Michif director Olivia Marie Golosky, a member of McMurray Métis Local 1935 from nistawâyâw (Fort McMurray, Alberta, Treaty 8).

films.title: The Adventures of Robin Hood
films.synopsis: We’re starting another year of Film Club with The Adventures of Robin Hood, one of the earliest (and best) films made using the eye-popping technology of three-strip Technicolor!

films.title: The Goddess of 1967
films.synopsis: Before Hollywood beckoned, a 20-year-old Rose Byrne was cast in Clara Law’s second Australian-made picture, an oneiric, Rubik’s Cube road movie The Goddess of 1967, which earned the up-and-comer Best Actress at Venice.

films.title: They Say the Moon Is Fuller Here
films.synopsis: The hallmarks of Clara Law’s diasporic cinema were firmly in place for her auspicious debut They Say the Moon Is Fuller Here, a 16mm graduation film that the study-abroad student fought to develop into a full-fledged feature.

films.title: Farewell, China
films.synopsis: The land of the free is but a purgatory for poor immigrants in Clara Law’s harrowing, post-Tiananmen Square drama Farewell, China.

films.title: The Heirloom
films.synopsis: A rescue dog tests the sturdiness of a modern millennial couple in Toronto-based filmmaker Ben Petrie’s long-awaited debut feature The Heirloom, a slippery, self-reflexive relationship comedy.

films.title: Autumn Moon
films.synopsis: Autumn Moon, winner of the Golden Leopard at Locarno, is arguably Clara Law’s chef d’oeuvre.

films.title: Inay
films.synopsis: In Inay (which means “mama” in Tagalog), Vancouver-based filmmaker Thea Loo compassionately examines the legacy of Canada’s Live-in Caregiver Program and its lasting psychological impact on the people closest to her.

films.title: The Sacrifice
films.synopsis: Armageddon is imminent in Russian mystic and master Andrei Tarkovsky’s final film The Sacrifice, described by the director as a meditation on ​“the absence in our culture of room for spiritual experience.”

films.title: Akira
films.synopsis: “Anime” entered the cultural lexicon in the West due in no small part to the impact of Akira, Otomo Katsuhiro’s retina-searing adaptation of his own manga series and one of the indelible exemplars of Japanese animation and the cyberpunk subgenre.

films.title: Gummo
films.synopsis: Named after the neglected Marx Brother, Korine’s calling-card masterpiece is a surreal, exhilaratingly nonlinear, non-judgemental snapshot of suburban ennui set in a strange, post-apocalyptic Xenia, Ohio.

films.title: Youth (Hard Times)
films.synopsis: Hard Times, the second installment of Wang Bing’s monumental Youth trilogy, resumes his immersive, observational chronicle of China’s garment-factory workers of the Zhili district.

films.title: The End of Summer
films.synopsis: The End of Summer, Ozu’s penultimate film, is a rich, ensemble family drama that follows a delicate trajectory from comedy to tragedy.

films.title: Floating Weeds
films.synopsis: Floating Weeds, a colour remake of his silent picture A Story of Floating Weeds, was produced for Daiei Film and made exceptional use of its stable of talent, most notably cinematographer Miyagawa Kazuo (Ugetsu).

films.title: Three Wishes for Cinderella
films.synopsis: Few fairy tale adaptations capture the renewable wonder of the many-times-told fable like the Czech yuletide classic Three Wishes for Cinderella.

films.title: Equinox Flower
films.synopsis: A gorgeously rendered work of great formal precision, Equinox Flower is Ozu’s first film in colour, a demand made by Shochiku to accentuate the beauty of star Yamamoto Fujiko, on loan from rival studio Daiei.

films.title: 3 Women
films.synopsis: In Robert Altman’s 3 Women, Millie (Shelley Duvall), Pinky (Sissy Spacek), and Willie (Janice Rule) are the mythic trinity of the title.

films.title: Thelma & Louise
films.synopsis: It begins like a 1940s women’s picture, runs through a patch of noir territory, and increasingly picks up the attributes of an Americana crime movie, but Thelma & Louise is first and foremost a dialogue.

films.title: The Umbrellas of Cherbourg
films.synopsis: Jacques Demy’s romantic musical The Umbrellas of Cherbourg is almost surely the first thing that comes to mind if you read the words “French musical in colour.” How could it not?

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.synopsis: A prestige period piece set in the high-end fashion world of 1950s London gradually gives way to a delectably perverse portrait of marriage in Paul Thomas Anderson’s masterful Phantom Thread, among the high points of American cinema last decade.

films.title: Mon oncle
films.synopsis: A marvellous satire in the spirit of Chaplin’s Modern Times, Mon oncle, Jacques Tati’s first colour film, won both a Special Jury Prize at Cannes and the Oscar for Best Foreign Language Film.

films.title: Good Morning
films.synopsis: Good Morning, Ozu’s 50th film and his second in colour, is a masterful comedy of manners about small talk and social niceties, set against 1950s suburbia and featuring flatulence as a major motif!

films.title: Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors
films.synopsis: Before Sergei Parajanov filtered desire and poetry through the symbolic tableaux of The Colour of Pomegranates, he made Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors, an equally stunning work of dynamic, mythic romantic fate.

films.title: Army of Shadows
films.synopsis: Army of Shadows, the most personal picture of Jean-Pierre Melville’s career, is an episodic epic of tragic, conflicted responsibility—whether to save others, survive, or perhaps to simply stick to a code.

films.title: Great Absence
films.synopsis: Set against the lush landscape of Omuto in southern Japan, Chika-ura’s sophomore feature Great Absence offers carefully composed cinematography and measured performances to deliver a suspenseful yet contemplative family drama.

films.title: Early Spring
films.synopsis: Early Spring, Ozu’s first-rate follow-up to 1953’s Tokyo Story, finds the director returning to a favourite milieu of his earlier, silent work: the workaday world of salaried office men.

films.title: Dorsky & Hiler: Program Two
films.synopsis: Dorsky and Hiler’s In the Stone House, 17 Reasons Why, and Song and Solitude each employ a starkly different method to arrive at the astonishing beauty found across their respective films.

films.title: Little Women
films.synopsis: More than any other version of Little Women, this film, directed by Gillian Armstrong (My Brilliant Career) and shot around British Columbia, emphasizes the adolescence of the March sisters as a rich association of emotionally potent memories.

films.title: Tokyo Story
films.synopsis: Tokyo Story is usually cited as Ozu’s supreme masterpiece, and is widely considered one of the greatest films ever made.

films.title: The Munekata Sisters
films.synopsis: Ozu’s first film produced outside of Shochiku was The Munekata Sisters, a rarely screened gem of a shoshimin-eiga (middle-class drama) about two sisters on opposite sides of the generational divide.

films.title: A Hen in the Wind
films.synopsis: Dismissed as a minor work—owing, one presumes, to its irreconcilable deviations and Ozu’s own misgivings about the film—A Hen in the Wind reveals a crucial, darker shade of the great director.

films.title: Record of a Tenement Gentleman
films.synopsis: Bittersweet, comedic, and fable-like, Record of a Tenement Gentleman is Ozu’s first postwar picture and his final collaboration with screenwriter Ikeda Tadao.

films.title: Late Spring
films.synopsis: Late Spring is one of Ozu’s finest achievements and a personal favourite of the director.

films.title: The Road Forward
films.synopsis: The Road Forward is a genre-blending musical documentary by award-winning Métis and Dene writer, director, playwright, and producer Marie Clements.

films.title: Dorsky & Hiler: Program One
films.synopsis: Hours for Jerome, likely Nathaniel Dorsky’s best-known yet too-rarely-seen work, screens as the centrepiece of this first of two nights of analogue film by Dorsky and his creative partner Jerome Hiler.

films.title: Alborz, We Climb Mountains
films.synopsis: Alborz, We Climb Mountains explores the history of Tehran’s legendary Alborz High School, a prestigious institution that has shaped generations of Iran’s brightest minds.

films.title: Every Man for Himself
films.synopsis: Described by Godard as “my second first film,” Every Man for Himself marked the director’s glorious return to “mainstream” cinema after years of video experimentation.

films.title: Weekend
films.synopsis: Godard’s astonishing 15th feature Weekend, one of his pinnacle achievements, closed the book on the New Wave stage of his career.

films.title: Far From Home
films.synopsis: Far From Home, one of three early films Saless co-wrote with Helga Houzer, his partner at the time, portrays a household of Turkish gastarbeiter in West Germany, showing both the misery of their isolation and moments of surprising warmth.

films.title: The Stranger and the Fog
films.synopsis: An unusually grand and mythic entry in the Iranian New Wave, The Stranger and the Fog is a film with the scale to match period epics by Kurosawa or Bergman.

films.title: Samsara
films.synopsis: Samsara is a sui generis cinematic experience, a hyper-immersive, transportative work suggestive of expanded, out-of-body cinema.

films.title: Toute une nuit
films.synopsis: New Restoration. The stillness of an Edward Hopper painting and the mounting progression of a structural film are married in Chantal Akerman’s Toute une nuit, a secret masterwork in which people meet, part, and reunite over a single hushed evening.

films.title: The Hardest Hour
films.synopsis: The EUFF continues to stand in solidarity with the people of Ukraine and their fight for self-determination. That struggle is chronicled in The Hardest Hour, a documentary consisting entirely of cellphone footage taken by Ukrainians on the ground.

films.title: Never Say Never
films.synopsis: This engaging romantic comedy revolves around Nadia (Tereza Kostková) and Peter (Tomáš Maštalír), whose paths accidentally cross—by way of a chance car collision—while both are struggling in failing marriages.

films.title: .dog
films.synopsis: In .dog, young Dimitris faces a profound and difficult dilemma: a choice between an unreliable, suspicious father and a family that says they want to adopt him.

films.title: Because I Love Bad Weather
films.synopsis: An absorbing tale of friendship, time passing, and second chances, writer-director Yana Lekarska’s debut feature film Because I Love Bad Weather captured the audience award at this year’s Sofia International Film Festival.

films.title: #annaismissing
films.synopsis: annaismissing, a shockingly eventful story about a missing influencer, follows a narrative that will change not only 15-year-old Nina’s life but also her family’s.

films.title: Mermaids Don’t Cry
films.synopsis: Mermaids Don’t Cry, a fanciful, imaginative comedy, revolves around Annika (Stefanie Reinsperger), a supermarket worker who dreams of the perfect synthetic mermaid fin.

films.title: A Postcard from Rome
films.synopsis: A Postcard from Rome, a sensitive, moving cinematic gem, was named Best Feature at the Latvian National Film Awards earlier this year.

films.title: Icarus and the Minotaur
films.synopsis: For Icarus and the Minotaur, his first feature as a director, Carlo Vogele delivers an entertaining film that reimagines the myth of Icarus as the story of a daydreaming little boy.

films.title: Hygge!
films.synopsis: Hygge!, a comedy from director Dagur Kári (Nói albinói) revolves around the title’s concept of creating joy and contentment in one’s everyday life in both humorous and revealing ways.

films.title: Salgueiro Maia: The Implicated
films.synopsis: The Implicated is an inspiring chronicle of Salgueiro Maia, one of that Carnation Revolution’s national heroes.

films.title: Verdigris
films.synopsis: Writer-director Patricia Kelly’s debut feature Verdigris revolves around an unlikely friendship between two very different women.

films.title: Family Therapy
films.synopsis: Family Therapy is a sharp, satirical, and trenchant examination of a complacent, rather crass Slovenian nouveau riche family from talented writer-director Sonja Presenc.

films.title: Freedom
films.synopsis: Tudor Giurgiu’s powerful historical drama Freedom is set in the provincial town of Sibiu, Romania, during the 1989 revolution.

films.title: The Invisible Fight
films.synopsis: The Invisible Fight, the long-awaited follow-up to director Rainer Sarnet’s freak-folk breakout November, is the strangest fun you will have in a cinema this year!

films.title: Cats of Malta
films.synopsis: More than just a film about cats, Cats of Malta is also about the power of community and the peculiar strength found within the human-cat connection, as well as its curious effects on Maltese life and culture.

films.title: Hilma
films.synopsis: Renowned director Lasse Hallström tells the extraordinary life story of Hilma af Klint, a Swedish artist who can now be seen as a revolutionary modernist—one of the first purely abstract painters—but who went largely unrecognized in her lifetime.

films.title: A Blue Flower
films.synopsis: Based on A play about Mirjana and those around her by award-winning playwright Ivor Martinić, A Blue Flower is a revealing exploration of the relationships between three generations of women.

films.title: One Million Minutes
films.synopsis: One Million Minutes, the directorial debut from longtime producer Christopher Doll, is a touching, amusing, profound, and unforgettable journey.

films.title: Dodo
films.synopsis: An absurdist film influenced by the work of Yorgos Lanthimos and other members of the “Greek Weird Wave,” Dodo is independent writer-director Panos H. Koutras’s fifth feature.

films.title: Rowing Across the Atlantic
films.synopsis: Rowing Across the Atlantic chronicles the incredible-but-true story of Aurimas Valujavičius, who embarked on one of the greatest challenges of his (or anyone’s) life: an 8500 kilometer solo journey across the Atlantic.

films.title: Scarborn
films.synopsis: A captivating historical drama, Scarborn triumphed at the 2024 Polish Film Awards, winning six awards including Best Directing, Screenplay, and Sound.

films.title: Frédéric Back 100
films.synopsis: The films of Frédéric Back (1924–2013) are tales of creation and destruction, rendering the epic and the eternal in gentle, impressionist pencil strokes

films.title: My Dad Is a Sausage
films.synopsis: In My Dad Is a Sausage, Paul leaves his boring but secure and well-paying job to finally fulfill his dream of becoming an actor, a quixotic quest that seems doomed to failure were it not for the support of his youngest daughter Zoë.

films.title: Fathers & Sons
films.synopsis: Reminiscent in style and tone to the Coen brothers, Fathers & Sons is a deliciously dark and droll comedy.

films.title: Light Light Light
films.synopsis: Alternating between past and present, Finnish director Inari Niemi delivers a sensitive, moving coming-of-age story in Light Light Light, skillfully navigating between Mariia’s sunny, hopeful youth and her sombre, challenging present.

films.title: Un amor
films.synopsis: Directed by the internationally acclaimed Isabel Coixet, Un amor, an adaptation of the novel by Sara Mesa, is a powerful, unsettling psychosexual drama.

films.title: A Hundred Sundays
films.synopsis: A Hundred Sundays follows a former shipyard worker, Antonio, who leads a quiet life until he needs to fight to make his—and his daughter Emilia’s—dream come true.

films.title: Boléro
films.synopsis: This sumptuous biographical drama directed by Anne Fontaine (Coco avant Chanel, The Innocents) captures the troubled composer Ravel in the process of devising Boléro, one of the most famous pieces of music ever written.

films.title: Some Birds
films.synopsis: Director Dániel Hevér’s promising debut feature film Some Birds is an uplifting story of friendship and boasts winning performances by László Szacsvay and Lilla Kizlinger.

films.title: The Stimming Pool
films.synopsis: Inspired by the concept of an “autistic camera,” The Stimming Pool is a brilliant exercise in collaborative filmmaking in which the lived experiences of several neurodiverse artists reign supreme.

films.title: Chris Welsby: Transmissions from the Natural World
films.synopsis: The Cinematheque and Cineworks Independent Filmmaking Society are pleased to welcome Chris Welsby for an evening devoted to his exceptional work.

films.title: Teachings for the noḵe', wahkohtowin, and t̲s̲'iyanne
films.synopsis: In anticipation of National Child Day, Our Stories to Tell presents this program of three stop-motion animation shorts imparting teachings: Məca, Wapos Bay: They Dance at Night, and Spirit Bear: Fishing for Knowledge, Catching Dreams.

films.title: Passion
films.synopsis: Passion is a visually sumptuous, bursting-with-ideas rumination on art and commerce (and, of course, filmmaking), featuring Jerzy Radziwilowicz as a Polish director trying to make a film based on masterpieces by Rembrandt, Goya, and Delacroix.

films.title: La Chinoise
films.synopsis: La Chinoise offers a spectacularly colourful pop-art portrait of five student radicals, members of a Maoist cell, who spend a summer in a Paris apartment chanting from Mao’s Little Red Book and plotting an assassination.

films.title: Anne at 13,000 ft
films.synopsis: Anchored by a roundly praised, powder-keg performance from Deragh Campbell, Kazik Radwanski’s triumphant third feature Anne at 13,000 ft stands as a highpoint in Anglo-Canada’s noise-making new wave.

films.title: Matt and Mara
films.synopsis: Canadian writer-director Kazik Radwanski follows up his breakthrough feature Anne at 13,000 ft with Matt and Mara, a piercing and disarmingly playful will-they-won’t-they drama.

films.title: Eureka
films.synopsis: The dilating effects of colonial violence in the Americas are artfully explored in Eureka, an ambitious new work from Argentine auteur Lisandro Alonso.

films.title: The Straight Story
films.synopsis: New Restoration. A pure distillation of David Lynch’s love for the sturdiness and uncanniness of people and things shaped by the American Midwest, The Straight Story is a film of single-minded purpose.

films.title: Häxan + Witch’s Cradle
films.synopsis: Our Halloween programs as part of “Watch Out, That Woman Has Evil Eyes: Celluloid Witches” begin with a reprise of Benjamin Christensen’s silent era masterpiece Häxan, preceded by Maya Deren’s posthumous work Witch’s Cradle.

films.title: The Witches
films.synopsis: Led by the legendary producer Dino De Laurentiis, The Witches brings together five renowned Italian auteurs—Visconti, Bolognini, Pasolini, Rossi, and de Sica—asking them to serve up their best witch.

films.title: Inferno
films.synopsis: Inferno, part two of Dario Argento’s witch trilogy, xpands the myth of The Three Mothers from Suspiria and reimagines the parameters of the horror cycle’s visually slick, surrealist universe.

films.title: The Blair Witch Project + Under Land
films.synopsis: The Blair Witch Project marked a new trend in genre filmmaking, making a case for low-budget productions fuelled by pure inventiveness.

films.title: I Married a Witch
films.synopsis: René Clair brings signature charm to I Married a Witch’s story of a father-daughter witch duo whose meddling in other people’s love lives starts backfiring in delightfully cheeky ways.

films.title: The Witches of Eastwick
films.synopsis: Mad Max’s very own George Miller flexes his witch muscle with The Witches of Eastwick, a deliciously twisted adaptation of John Updike’s 1984 novel starring Cher, Michelle Pfeiffer, and Susan Sarandon.

films.title: Häxan
films.synopsis: Häxan stages an exaggerated retelling of the practice of witchcraft through the ages, piecing together original writings, paintings, illustrations, and witness accounts to bring to life the essence of the witch myth.

films.title: Lyana Patrick: People of the Land
films.synopsis: To mark one year of Our Stories to Tell, we’re celebrating series advisor Lyana Patrick with a program consisting of three of her short films, followed by Luke Gleeson’s DƏNE YI’INJETL – The Scattering of Man.

films.title: Fleischer Cartoons × 10
films.synopsis: The Fleischer brothers Max (producer) and Dave (director) made many of the wildest technical leaps in the early years of animation, as seen in these shorts, which star Betty Boop, Popeye the Sailor, and Clark Kent!

films.title: SFMF Series 2
films.synopsis: Films selected for this program foreground procedural operations between different orders of magnitude while endlessly crystallizing surfaces and framing devices as forms of life.

films.title: SFMF Series 3
films.synopsis: Drifting off into space but longing for the earth, these films evoke peripheral visions, sacred distortions, and supernatural intimacies of the everyday.

films.title: SFMF Series 1
films.synopsis: Twelve short films open the festival, starting with a frustrated keystroke execution extracting an endless feed of stray thoughts, entrenched narratives, and analogue connections that stretch out before collapsing into infinity.

films.title: Exodus
films.synopsis: Exodus, set in an immigrant processing centre, examines the bureaucratic processes faced by Afghan migrants attempting to leave Iran amidst economic collapse.

films.title: After Work
films.synopsis: Weaving together expert analysis by sociologists and philosophers with testimonials from everyday workers worldwide, After Work explores the existential and material paradoxes of modern work culture.

films.title: Cluny Brown
films.synopsis: After meeting over a clogged kitchen sink, writer Adam Belinski (Charles Boyer) and plumbing apprentice Cluny Brown (Jennifer Jones) find themselves at the mercy of a wealthy family.

films.title: To Be or Not to Be
films.synopsis: The deeply serious and profoundly ridiculous mingle in To Be or Not to Be, surely one of Ernst Lubitsch’s finest, most spectacular comedies.

films.title: One Hour with You
films.synopsis: One Hour with You might be the most formally audacious of Lubitsch’s musicals. A rare tinted talkie, it includes scenes delivered in couplets and audience-complicit asides that shatter the fourth wall.

films.title: The Human Surge 3
films.synopsis: Williams’s medium-pushing film The Human Surge 3 is a work as equally attuned to the present as to future presents imminent but not yet arrived.

films.title: Lady Windermere’s Fan + I Don’t Want to Be a Man
films.synopsis: “Only Lubitsch Could Have Made It” begins with a double-bill of silent works by the director. I Don't Want to Be a Man, a German mid-length is followed by Lady Windermere’s Fan, an American feature-length adaptation of Oscar Wilde’s play.

films.title: Angel
films.synopsis: One might call Angel Lubitsch’s deepest undercover work: everything is related in pure hypotheticals and productive ambiguity, leaving romance and reality in suspense.

films.title: Goodbye to Language + Les trois désastres
films.synopsis: JLG plus 3D adds up, little wonder, to two of the most radical, rule-bending reimaginings in the history of the technique. Goodbye to Language and Les trois désastres will screen with key Godard 3D collaborator Fabrice Aragno in attendance.

films.title: Scénarios + Exposé du film annonce du film “Scénario”
films.synopsis: Godard’s final films make their Vancouver premiere in this special presentation. Scénarios and Exposé du film annonce du film “Scénario” screen with key collaborator Fabrice Aragno in person, followed by Aragno’s Quod erat demonstrandum.

films.title: 2 or 3 Things I Know About Her
films.synopsis: Amy Taubin calls 2 or 3 Things I Know About Her “the greatest film by the greatest post-1950s filmmaker.” She’s not alone in considering it the auteur’s chef-d’oeuvre.

films.title: First Name: Carmen
films.synopsis: Godard took top prize at Venice for First Name: Carmen, an erotically charged tragicomedy, gorgeously lensed by his New Wave cinematographer Raoul Coutard in their final collaboration.

films.title: Histoire(s) du cinéma
films.synopsis: Histoire(s) du cinéma, a massive, meditative, mosaic history of cinema, is undeniably one of Godard’s supreme achievements—for many, his magnum opus.

films.title: Hail Mary
films.synopsis: Godard’s startling modernization of the Annunciation and Nativity stories depicts the Virgin Mary as the basketball-playing daughter of a gas-station manager, and Joseph as her jealous taxi-driver boyfriend.

films.title: Moving
films.synopsis: A work of astonishing assurance of vision, anchored by a dazzling child performance from Tabata Tomoko, Moving distills the strong points of Somai’s filmmaking, while folding a subtler, more restrained poeticism into its approach.

films.title: Masculin féminin
films.synopsis: Godard is in full Godardian glory in the marvellous Masculin féminin, a disquisition on youth, sex, politics, sexual politics, and pop culture presented as a 15-point investigation into “the children of Marx and Coca-Cola.”

films.title: The Hearing
films.synopsis: The Hearing explores the hidden world of asylum hearings—the process that millions of refugees worldwide experience when applying for citizenship in a new country.

films.title: Seven Samurai
films.synopsis: Celebrating 70 years, Kurosawa Akira’s stirring epic Seven Samurai receives an anniversary restoration befitting its status as one of international cinema’s immovable masterworks.

films.title: Sirocco and the Kingdom of Winds
films.synopsis: Sirocco and the Kingdom of Winds, the new and slightly surreal tale from directing-writing duo Benoît Chieux and Alain Gagnol (A Cat in Paris), is a hand-drawn wonder over ten years in the making!

films.title: Abuse of Weakness
films.synopsis: After chronicling the experience of being a victim of fraud in a book, Catherine Breillat made Abuse of Weakness, a uniquely haunting film with a bold, tough performance by Isabelle Huppert at its centre.

films.title: Act of Violence
films.synopsis: WWII shadows the entire noir cycle, but it rarely receives as thorough an exorcism as it does in Act of Violence, the sole entry in the genre by director Fred Zinnemann (From Here to Eternity).

films.title: The Sleeping Beauty
films.synopsis: The Sleeping Beauty, a constantly surprising, thought-provoking investigation of the female psyche, reaffirmed Breillat as one of the most inventive and risk-taking of contemporary French auteurs.

films.title: T-Men
films.synopsis: T-Men, the first collaboration between key noir image-makers John Alton and Anthony Mann, is a mob-financed, fed-approved barn burner that, in case you’re having too much fun, pauses for sober “voice of god” narration!

films.title: Alphaville
films.synopsis: Godard’s Alphaville, a hugely influential mix of science fiction and film noir, was originally called Tarzan vs. IBM, a title suggestive of its theme and its pop-art/pulp-fiction sensibilities.

films.title: Détective
films.synopsis: A mainstream genre picture sacrificed on the altar of late Godard, Détective is a cunning, comic deconstruction of the film noir, calculatedly timed to intersect with our summer cycle of American screen pulp.

films.title: The Wanderer
films.synopsis: Comprising seven narrative sequences, The Wanderer is based on a script by artist Rory Macbeth who, without any knowledge of German, translated Franz Kafka’s The Metamorphosis into English.

films.title: Bluebeard
films.synopsis: Bluebeard, Catherine Breillat’s faithfully pitch-black take on the perverse Charles Perrault folktale, casts a haunting spell with its formal austerity and savage lessons on innocence lost.

films.title: Liz and the Blue Bird
films.synopsis: To celebrate the occasion of Yamada Naoko’s first new film in six years, we’re presenting Liz and the Blue Bird, a beautifully sensitive portrayal of school-age confidence, in all its halting ups and downs.

films.title: The Last Mistress
films.synopsis: After plumbing the depths of depravity in Anatomy of Hell, Breillat returned to the industry’s good graces with The Last Mistress, her elegant, erotic adaptation of Jules Barbey d’Aurevilly’s 1851 novel of the same name.

films.title: White Heat
films.synopsis: Categorized in Paul Schrader’s noir dossier as “post-hardboiled,” Raoul Walsh’s White Heat, an extremely controlled film about an intemperate anti-hero, is one of the masterpieces of American cinema.

films.title: The Unseen
films.synopsis: The Unseen, the feature-length debut of Behzad Nalbandi, premiered at the International Documentary Film Festival Amsterdam (IDFA). It depicts the lives of those who dwell in public spaces and streets, overlooked by society as if invisible.

films.title: The Man I Love
films.synopsis: The partnership of director Raoul Walsh and actor Ida Lupino culminated in The Man I Love, starring Lupino as Petey Brown, a New Yorker who knows the late-night solace and high-pressure drive of the music scene, but hasn’t yet tasted success.

films.title: Neige
films.synopsis: Neige, a coolly committed piece of street poetry, was Juliet Berto’s directorial debut, a tale of two guardian angels and the heavily policed community—dealers, users, dancers, lovers, and friends—they watch over.

films.title: Sex Is Comedy
films.synopsis: Sex Is Comedy is a self-mocking film à clef. More precisely, a Breillat-like director, shooting a movie like Breillat’s Fat Girl, faced with the difficulty of putting convincing sizzle in a key sex scene between two actors who loathe each other.

films.title: Anatomy of Hell
films.synopsis: An aggressive polemic on male terror of the female body, Anatomy of Hell is a bracing, brooding film based on Breillat’s 2001 novel Pornocracy.

films.title: Beans
films.synopsis: Beans is the debut narrative feature of Mohawk filmmaker Tracey Deer. Based on Deer’s real-life experiences of the Oka Crisis, this semi-autobiographical film channels her memories through the character of Tekehentahkhwa, also known as Beans.

films.title: Victims of Sin
films.synopsis: As a classic example of the cine de rumberas genre, Victims of Sin is a jolting rediscovery, mixing high-level craft and underworld action.

films.title: Pickup on South Street
films.synopsis: Pickup on South Street’s plot concerns the contents of the purse belonging to Candy (Jean Peters) that professional thief Skip (Richard Widmark) has recently grabbed: microfilm en route to a communist spy, courtesy of Candy’s opportunist ex.

films.title: To Have and Have Not
films.synopsis: To Have and Have Not, the first onscreen pairing of Lauren Bacall and Humphrey Bogart, is part Casablanca, part Hawksian worldview, total hard-edged brilliance.

films.title: My Heart Is That Eternal Rose
films.synopsis: Patrick Tam’s final—and possibly definitive—statement as a member of the Hong Kong New Wave is My Heart Is That Eternal Rose, a pulpy and audacious heroic bloodshed film.
